forensicMd:
## Thesis

BACH (PT BACH Multi Global Tbk) is a fast-growing, cheaply-priced genset and telecom-infrastructure company whose IPO is wrapped around a control hand-over from the founding Bach family (via holding company BMSI) to the Hartono family (via PT Global Telekomunikasi Prima, GTP). The listing itself raises Rp246.0bn–Rp307.5bn in primary capital, but it sits on top of a larger, parallel transaction: a pre-agreed option that lifts GTP from 30% to 51% within five working days of listing, structured to avoid a mandatory tender offer. The investable question is not who controls BACH — clearly the Hartono group post-listing — but whether the new controller runs it for all shareholders or as a captive vendor to its tower empire. The economics for minorities hinge on whether you trust the Hartono ecosystem to feed BACH on arm's-length terms, and on a stock that prices in real cyclical, leverage, and governance risk at roughly 10–13x trailing earnings.

## How the business works

BACH operates two lines: genset (generator) sale and rental, which generated 56.4% of 2025 revenue, and telecom-infrastructure construction and maintenance through its 99.99% subsidiary PT Bach Multi Infrastruktur (est. 2019), at 43.6%. This is a capital-intensive trading and rental model: the company imports gensets (notably from Guangdong Westinpower, China) and either sells them or deploys them into a rental fleet, while the infra arm builds and maintains telecom-tower sites. Two dependencies define the business. First, supply: the prospectus names reliance on Chinese genset suppliers as its top risk, carrying FX and supply-chain exposure. Second, customer concentration: BACH's demand is anchored to a handful of tower clients — chiefly Protelindo (TOWR group) and PT Solusi Tunas Pratama (IDX: SUPR) — each around 14% of 2025 revenue, with Protelindo having been as high as 40% in 2024. Crucially, Protelindo belongs to the same Hartono group taking control, so a meaningful slice of revenue converts into related-party revenue post-listing. The demand backdrop is genuinely supportive: Indonesia's data-centre, telecom-tower, and electrification build-out is driving genset demand, and 2025's surge was almost entirely a genset-rental story tied to that capex cycle — powerful now, but cyclical and supplier-dependent.

## The offering & ownership

The IPO is 615,000,000 new shares (15.06% of post-IPO capital), all primary, with no secondary tranche — so every rupiah of the Rp246.0bn–Rp307.5bn gross raise accrues to the company. Pricing is Rp400–Rp500, implying a post-IPO market cap of roughly Rp1.63tn–Rp2.04tn on 4,084,430,000 shares. The float sits at the 15.06% regulatory floor even though the RUPS authorised up to 25%, making the thin liquidity a deliberate choice (and a stated investment risk). The signature structural feature is the option: on 7 January 2026 GTP and BMSI signed an irrevocable option, and on 13 March 2026 GTP gave notice to exercise, buying 1,042,227,300 BMSI shares via a negotiated-market crossing within five working days of listing (around 14 July 2026). That control sale is worth roughly Rp417bn–Rp521bn — larger than the entire IPO raise — and its strike is set after bookbuilding, meaning the same book that prices the public's shares also prices the Bach family's exit. BMSI does not fully exit; it retains 26.77%, so this is a control sale, not a clean exit, leaving the public's 15.06% beneath a 51% Hartono controller and a 26.77% Bach legacy holder. On lock-up: the prospectus asserts no POJK 25/2017 eight-month lock-up applies (claiming no shareholder acquired shares below the IPO price in the six months before registration — worth verifying against a February 2026 par-price cash subscription by Zulfahmi Fithri); the only contractual lock is GTP's own declaration (13 April 2026) not to relinquish control for at least 12 months after effectiveness. The five Bach founders are unlocked, and there is no ESA or ESOP.

## Governance & related parties

The defining governance issue is related-party concentration converging with board interlock. The incoming ultimate beneficial owners are named in the prospectus as Martin Basuki Hartono and Victor Rachmat Hartono (Hartono family — Djarum / BCA / GDP Venture), whose group also controls Sarana Menara Nusantara (IDX: TOWR) and Protelindo, and whose orbit includes SUPR as a cross-readable listed tower peer. BACH's chair, Komut Anita Anwar, is concurrently VP Director of Protelindo, Director of TOWR, and Komut of Remala Abadi (IDX: REMALA) — meaning the chair sits on both sides of BACH's largest customer relationship. Post-acquisition, roughly 14–40% of revenue becomes related-party revenue: analytically, this cuts both ways as captive demand (a potential moat) versus transfer-pricing exposure for minorities. The board carries only one independent commissioner of three (33%), meeting the 30% floor with no margin of safety, while Bach founders retain operating roles for management continuity as the Hartono group takes economic control. The most contested point is the mandatory-tender-offer question: the prospectus states the option exercise does not trigger an MTO under POJK 9/2018 because GTP was already "the controller" — a status established by shareholder resolution (Akta No. 32) on 8 January 2026, one day after the option agreement, when GTP held only 30% against BMSI's 61.55%. As analysis, designating a 30% holder as controller days before exercising to 51% is a recognised but aggressive structure that denies public minorities a tender-offer exit at the control price; the harm is limited if the stock holds at or above the IPO price but becomes real if it trades below. The legal record is otherwise clean — neither the company, its subsidiary, nor its directors and commissioners are party to any legal dispute.

## Reading the financials

The trajectory is the inverse of a tired business. Revenue grew roughly 73% over two years to Rp1.73tn in 2025 (+39.66%), gross margin expanded to 18.0%, and net profit compounded from Rp35.4bn (2023) to Rp155.6bn (2025) — up about 340% — lifting net margin to 9.0% and ROE to 29.02%. Non-controlling interest is negligible, so attributable profit roughly equals total. The growth driver is genset, not telecom: genset revenue more than doubled to Rp978.0bn (+117%) in 2025 while telecom-infra revenue slipped slightly, so the thesis is effectively a genset-rental cyclical bet tied to tower and data-centre capex. The cost of that growth shows on the balance sheet. 2025 capex of Rp369.7bn (the rental fleet) nearly quadrupled non-current assets to Rp465bn and was funded largely by Rp206bn of net new borrowing, pushing total-liabilities DER to 1.30x and leaving only Rp11.97bn of year-end cash against Rp595bn of current liabilities. Operating cash flow is positive and improving (Rp132bn) but is dwarfed by the fleet spend — as analysis, this is a company outrunning its own cash flow: fine while demand is hot, dangerous if the genset cycle turns, and the IPO equity is precisely what relieves the squeeze. A pro-forma de-lever to roughly 0.75x is plausible after the raise and the Rp91bn revolver repayment, but it is a labelled estimate (a clean interest-bearing DER needs the Permata-plus-SMBC bank-debt detail) and BACH will likely re-lever to keep buying gensets. One pattern worth stating plainly: cash dividends of Rp129.1bn in 2023 — about 3.6x that year's net profit, funded from retained earnings — drained accumulated earnings to the Bach owners shortly before bringing in GTP and the public.

## Valuation

On a post-money, attributable basis, BACH prices at roughly 10.5x trailing FY2025 earnings at Rp400 and 13.1x at Rp500 (EPS basis around Rp38.08), with P/B near 2.1x–2.4x and a rough EV/EBITDA of about 7–9x. For a business that grew net profit near 100% a year at 29% ROE, that is undemanding in isolation — for context, the source notes JEC asked 53–62x. As analysis, the discount is the market pricing the stack of risks: cyclicality, rising leverage, customer concentration, related-party governance, and the no-MTO control transfer. Two methodology caveats carry through: any peer comparison should be benchmarked against live multiples of TOWR, SUPR, and listed genset/EPC peers rather than stale figures, and because BACH will increasingly be valued on Hartono-ecosystem captive demand, TOWR's own capex trajectory is the leading indicator to watch. On the proceeds, this reads as a genuine growth-capital IPO: even at the floor price, the majority (63%, rising to 70.4% at the ceiling) buys revenue-generating genset inventory and rental fleet, with the balance repaying the Bank Permata revolver — more investor-friendly than a deleveraging-heavy deal, though the "growth" is still more inventory in a capital-intensive, supplier-dependent model at trading-business margins.

## Bottom line

BACH is two deals in one wrapper: a cheaply-priced, fast-growing genset/infra business raising real growth capital, and a control monetisation in which the Bach family sells command of the company to the Hartono group at a price the IPO book itself sets — with no tender offer for minorities and a sidecar 15.06% float. The bull case is concrete: ~10–13x earnings for ~100% profit growth and 29% ROE, a deep-pocketed strategic controller, captive demand from the Hartono tower ecosystem, growth-oriented use of proceeds, positive operating cash flow, a clean legal record, and a structural electrification tailwind. The bear case is equally concrete: a control transfer engineered around the MTO rules, related-party revenue overseen by a conflicted chair, debt-funded growth on a razor-thin cash base, dependence on Chinese genset supply, a cyclical demand engine, and a concentrated register where minority influence is minimal. The cheapness is real, but so is the reason for it — this is a bet that the Hartono group runs BACH for all shareholders rather than as a captive vendor, and the unresolved option strike price is the first datapoint that will reveal which way that goes.

narrativeMd:
# KUPAS PROSPEKTUS — BACH (PT BACH Multi Global Tbk)

> Source: Prospektus Awal (preliminary), dated 22 June 2026, 403 pp. Lead underwriter: PT Erdikha Elit Sekuritas (full commitment; co-underwriters TBD). Status: registration submitted to OJK, **not yet effective**. All figures cross-checked against the prospectus; analytical opinions are explicitly labelled **[OPINI]**.

---

## 0. SNAPSHOT

| Item | Detail |
|---|---|
| Issuer | PT BACH Multi Global Tbk (brand: BACH Group) |
| Ticker | **BACH** (intended) |
| Sector | Genset (generator) **sale + rental** and **telecom-infrastructure construction & maintenance** |
| Segments | Genset 56.4% of 2025 revenue; Telecom infra 43.6% |
| Subsidiary | PT Bach Multi Infrastruktur (BMI), 99.99%, est. 2019 — telecom-infra arm |
| Nominal value | Rp50/share (post 1:2,000 split from Rp100,000) |
| Price range | **Rp400 – Rp500** |
| Shares offered | **615,000,000 (15.06% of post-IPO)** — **all primary/new**, no secondary |
| Gross raise | **Rp246.0bn – Rp307.5bn** (excl. issuance cost) |
| ESA / ESOP | **None** |
| Post-IPO shares | 4,084,430,000 |
| Post-IPO market cap | **≈ Rp1.63tn – Rp2.04tn** |
| **Concurrent control transfer** | **GTP buys 1,042,227,300 shares from BMSI → 51% control within 5 days of listing** (price set by IPO book) |
| **Incoming controller / UBO** | **PT Global Telekomunikasi Prima (GTP)** / **Martin Basuki Hartono & Victor Rachmat Hartono (Hartono family)** |
| Indicative timeline | Bookbuilding 22–24 Jun; effective 29 Jun; offering 1–3 Jul; **listing 7 Jul 2026** |
| Top customer | PT Profesional Telekomunikasi Indonesia (Protelindo / TOWR group — Hartono) |

> **The one-line thesis [OPINI]:** a fast-growing, cheaply-priced genset/telecom-infra company whose IPO is wrapped around a **control hand-over from the Bach family (BMSI) to the Hartono family (GTP)** — structured to avoid a mandatory tender offer. The economics for minorities hinge on whether you trust the Hartono ecosystem to feed BACH on arm's-length terms.

---

## 1. CURRENT SHAREHOLDERS

**Pre-IPO issued capital:** 3,469,430,000 shares (Rp173,471,500,000) of authorized 12,800,000,000 (Rp640bn).

### Pre-IPO cap table
| Shareholder | Shares | % |
|---|---:|---:|
| **PT Bach Multi Sukses Investama (BMSI)** — Bach holding co. | 2,135,496,000 | **61.55%** |
| **PT Global Telekomunikasi Prima (GTP)** — Hartono | 1,040,832,000 | **30.00%** |
| Budi Kurniawan (Dir. Utama) | 69,600,000 | 2.01% |
| Hartanto Rahardja (Komisaris) | 62,400,000 | 1.80% |
| Edy Surianto | 55,200,000 | 1.59% |
| Zulfahmi Fithri | 53,102,000 | 1.53% |
| Hasby Jap (Direktur) | 52,800,000 | 1.52% |

The five named individuals (8.45%) are the original Bach founders/management; several remain on the board (see §7).

### Three ownership states (the core story)
| Bloc | Pre-IPO | Post-IPO (pre-option) | **Post-option (final)** |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **GTP (Hartono)** | 30.00% | 25.48% | **51.00%** |
| **BMSI (Bach)** | 61.55% | 52.28% | **26.77%** |
| Bach founders (5 indiv.) | 8.45% | 7.18% | 7.18% |
| Public | 0% | 15.06% | 15.06% |

**Things you must not miss:**
- **Control flips from Bach to Hartono.** BMSI falls 61.55% → 26.77%; GTP rises 30% → 51%. BMSI does **not** fully exit — it keeps 26.77% (worth ~Rp437–547bn) — so this is a **control sale, not a clean exit**.
- The **ultimate controllers are named in the prospectus as Martin Basuki Hartono and Victor Rachmat Hartono** (Hartono family — Djarum / BCA / GDP Venture).

---

## 2. AFFILIATION WITH THE EXISTING STOCK MARKET

| Channel | Detail | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Incoming controller = Hartono group** | GTP is Hartono-controlled; the family also controls **Sarana Menara Nusantara (IDX: TOWR)** and **Protelindo** (towers) | BACH is being absorbed into the **Hartono telecom-infrastructure ecosystem** |
| **Largest customer = Protelindo (TOWR group)** | 14% of 2025 revenue (40% in 2024); MSA signed 6 May 2025 | Currently arm's-length 3rd party; **becomes a related party once GTP takes control** |
| **#2 customer = PT Solusi Tunas Pratama (IDX: SUPR)** | 14% of 2025 revenue | Listed tower co.; cross-readable |
| **Board interlock** | Komut **Anita Anwar** is concurrently **VP Director of Protelindo**, **Director of TOWR**, and **Komut of Remala Abadi (IDX: REMALA)** | The chair sits on **both sides** of BACH's largest customer relationship |
| Underwriter | PT Erdikha Elit Sekuritas | — |

> 🚩 **RED FLAG [OPINI] — related-party concentration is the defining risk.** BACH's biggest customer (Protelindo) belongs to the same family that is taking control, and BACH's own chairman is a senior officer of that customer. Post-acquisition, **~14–40% of revenue becomes related-party revenue**. Cuts both ways: captive demand (a moat) vs. transfer-pricing exposure for minorities.

---

## 3. PUBLIC OFFERING & PROCEEDS

### Structure
- **615,000,000 new shares = 15.06%**, all primary → **all proceeds go to the company.** No secondary tranche in the IPO itself.
- Price range **Rp400–500**; gross raise **Rp246.0bn–Rp307.5bn**.

### The bigger, parallel transaction — the Option (BMSI → GTP)
- On **7 Jan 2026** GTP and BMSI signed an **irrevocable Option**; on **13 Mar 2026** GTP gave notice to exercise, buying **1,042,227,300 BMSI shares**.
- Settlement: a **negotiated-market crossing** within **5 working days of listing**; **price set after bookbuilding**.
- **Value of the control sale: ~Rp417bn–Rp521bn** (1,042,227,300 × Rp400–500) — **larger than the entire IPO raise.**

> 🚩 **RED FLAG — the IPO price directly sets the Bach family's exit price.** The option strike is "to be determined after bookbuilding," so the same book that prices the public's shares also prices BMSI's ~Rp417–521bn sale to GTP. **[OPINI]** That is a structural alignment question: the controllers benefit from a higher IPO print, and the deal's largest cash flow (the control sale) runs *outside* the IPO proceeds.

### Free float
- **15.06%** — at the regulatory floor; thin liquidity (the prospectus's stated investment risk). The RUPS authorised up to 25%, so the small float is a deliberate choice.

---

## 4. LOCK-UP, ESA / ESOP

### Lock-up
- 🚩 **No POJK 25/2017 8-month lock-up applies** — the prospectus asserts no shareholder acquired shares below the IPO price in the 6 months before registration. *(Worth verifying against the Feb-2026 cash subscription by Zulfahmi Fithri at par — see §7 open questions.)*
- The **only contractual lock-up** is GTP's: as direct controller, GTP declared (13 Apr 2026) it will **not relinquish control for ≥12 months** after effectiveness.
- **Implication:** BMSI's sale to GTP is *pre-planned and permitted* within 5 days of listing; the five founders are unlocked. Only GTP's *control* (not its full stake) is locked.

### ESA / ESOP
- **None.** No employee stock allocation or option programme — simpler than peers, but also no employee alignment mechanism.

---

## 5. USE OF IPO FUNDS — *growth-oriented*

**All proceeds accrue to the company.** Stated allocation (at the Rp307.5bn ceiling, net of issuance cost):

| # | Use | Amount | Nature |
|---|---|---:|---|
| 1 | Repay **Bank Permata** Omnibus Revolving Loan (working-capital facility, drawn Jan–Mar 2026) | Rp91,020,000,000 | Deleverage |
| 2 | **Genset purchase** — inventory for sale + rental fleet (e.g. 132 units from Guangdong Westinpower, China) | Rp213,480,000,000 | **Growth capital** |

- Permata facility balance Rp210.07bn (31 May 2026) → Rp119.05bn after repayment; rate 6.3%; non-affiliated.
- Genset suppliers are **non-affiliated**; the purchase is an **ordinary-course / exempted transaction** under POJK 17/2020.

> **[OPINI] — this is a genuine growth-capital IPO.** ~69% buys revenue-generating assets (genset inventory + rental fleet); ~30% repays a recent revolver. Contrast with deleveraging-heavy IPOs. The honest caveat: the "growth" is **more inventory in a capital-intensive, supplier-dependent trading/rental model** — it scales revenue but at trading-business margins, and it deepens reliance on Chinese genset supply.

---

## 6. CURRENT COMPANY PERFORMANCE

### Income statement (Rp)
| | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Revenue | 1,004,155,159,435 | 1,240,838,719,683 | 1,732,944,872,499 |
| Revenue growth | −5.15% | +23.57% | **+39.66%** |
| Gross profit | 160,805,909,297 | 198,017,973,708 | 311,433,904,623 |
| Gross margin | 16.0% | 16.0% | **18.0%** |
| Net profit | 35,421,014,510 | 78,742,995,252 | 155,550,910,711 |
| Net profit growth | −49.29% | +122.31% | **+97.54%** |
| Net margin | 3.5% | 6.3% | **9.0%** |
| EBITDA (≈) | 82.2bn | 124.5bn | **250.8bn** |
| ROE | 9.53% | 18.14% | **29.02%** |
| ROA | 5.21% | 9.65% | 12.62% |

**This is the inverse of a tired business:** revenue +73% over two years, net profit **+340%** (Rp35bn → Rp156bn), margins expanding, ROE at 29%. NCI is negligible, so attributable profit ≈ total.

> **Growth driver — genset, not telecom [data].** Genset revenue **doubled**: Rp450.9bn (2024) → **Rp978.0bn (2025), +117%**, while telecom-infra revenue *fell* slightly (Rp790bn → Rp755bn). The surge tracks the Rp369.7bn rental-fleet capex and Indonesia's data-centre/electrification boom. **[OPINI]** The thesis is therefore a **genset-rental cyclical bet** tied to tower/data-centre capex — powerful now, but cyclical and supplier-dependent.

### Balance sheet & cash flow (Rp)
| | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Total assets | 680,277,911,372 | 815,608,114,271 | 1,232,630,823,230 |
| Non-current assets | 100,434,130,194 | 132,264,402,454 | **465,391,834,564** |
| Current liabilities | 290,674,627,079 | 363,015,902,146 | **595,184,402,062** |
| Total equity | 371,823,002,600 | 434,013,666,717 | 535,953,925,981 |
| **DER (total liab ÷ equity)** | 0.83 | 0.88 | **1.30** |
| CFO | (40,906,529,131) | 65,403,622,884 | 131,883,498,360 |
| CFI | 2,090,854,624 | (37,179,920,079) | **(357,801,225,749)** |
| CFF | 21,470,374,698 | (19,553,854,990) | **205,889,278,427** |
| **Cash, year-end** | 23,233,102,394 | 31,905,682,325 | **11,971,028,627** |

> 🚩 **RED FLAG — debt-funded growth on a razor-thin cash base.** 2025 capex of **Rp369.7bn** (rental fleet; non-current assets nearly quadrupled to Rp465bn) was funded by **+Rp206bn of net borrowing** (CFF), pushing **DER to 1.30×** and leaving only **Rp11.97bn cash** against Rp595bn current liabilities. CFO (Rp132bn) is positive and improving, but is dwarfed by the fleet spend. **[OPINI]** Execution/liquidity risk is real; the IPO equity is what relieves it. This is a company outrunning its own cash flow — fine while demand is hot, dangerous if the genset cycle turns.

> 🚩 **RED FLAG — pre-IPO dividend extraction.** Cash dividends paid: **Rp129.1bn (2023)** — ~3.6× that year's Rp35.4bn net profit — then Rp17.7bn (2024) and Rp50.0bn (2025). The 2023 payout drained accumulated retained earnings to the Bach owners shortly before bringing in GTP and the public. Less aggressive than a debt-funded recap (these were funded from retained earnings), but the pattern — **extract, sell control, then IPO** — is worth stating plainly.

### Customer concentration (>10% of revenue)

|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **Protelindo** (TOWR group — Hartono) | 21% | **40%** | 14% |
| **PT Solusi Tunas Pratama (SUPR)** | 3% | 3% | 14% |
| **Top-2 combined** | 24% | **43%** | 28% |

> 🚩 **RED FLAG — concentration + related-party overlap.** Reliance on a few tower clients, the largest of which is Hartono-affiliated and becomes a **related party** post-control-transfer (§2).

## 7. ADDITIONAL — governance, valuation, dividend, risks, legal

### The mandatory-tender-offer question (POJK 9/2018)
The prospectus states the option exercise **does not trigger a mandatory tender offer**, because **"GTP is already the controller"** — a status established by **shareholder resolution (Akta No. 32) on 8 Jan 2026**, one day after the Option Agreement (7 Jan 2026), with GTP holding only 30% while BMSI held 61.55%.

> 🚩 **RED FLAG [OPINI] — MTO avoidance.** Designating a 30% holder as "controller" days before exercising an option to 51% is a recognised but aggressive structure that **denies public minorities a tender-offer exit at the control price.** The economic harm is limited *if* the stock holds at/above the IPO price (the likely option strike), but becomes real if it trades below — minorities then have no put to GTP. Probe the control basis (board/shareholders'-agreement) and whether OJK has blessed the no-MTO position.

### Governance / board
**Komisaris:** Anita Anwar (Komut — **Protelindo VP Director / TOWR Director / Remala Komut**); Daniel Gunawan (Independen); Hartanto Rahardja (also 1.80% holder).
**Direksi:** Budi Kurniawan (Dir. Utama, 2.01% holder); Hasby Jap (1.52% holder); Julius Irwandi; Irvan Rianto; Audia Michael Septian.
> **1 of 3 independent commissioners (33%)** — meets the 30% floor but no margin of safety, and the chair is conflicted via the largest customer. Bach founders retain operating roles (management continuity) while Hartono takes economic control.

### Valuation (post-money, attributable basis)
| Metric | At Rp400 | At Rp500 |
|---|---:|---:|
| Market cap | Rp1.63tn | Rp2.04tn |
| **Trailing P/E (FY2025, EPS ≈ Rp38.08)** | **10.5×** | **13.1×** |
| P/B (post-money) | ~2.1× | ~2.4× |
| EV/EBITDA (rough) | ~7–8× | ~9× |

> **[OPINI] — the pricing is undemanding.** ~10–13× trailing P/E for a business that grew net profit ~100%/yr at 29% ROE is *cheap* in isolation (JEC, by contrast, asked 53–62×). The discount is the market pricing the risks: cyclicality, leverage, concentration, related-party governance, and the no-MTO control transfer. **Methodology note:** benchmark against **live** multiples of TOWR, SUPR, and any listed genset/EPC peers before the deck — don't anchor to stale numbers. A key cross-check: BACH will be valued partly on Hartono-ecosystem captive demand, so TOWR's own capex trajectory is the leading indicator.

### Dividend policy
Up to **50% payout** from 2026 net profit, starting 2027. (Pre-IPO dividends already discussed in §6.)

### Key risk factors (per prospectus + analyst)
Stated #1: **dependency on genset suppliers** (Chinese supply concentration). Add: customer concentration / related-party pricing; rising leverage + thin liquidity; genset-cycle/data-centre dependence; share illiquidity (15% float); no-MTO control structure; FX exposure on imported gensets.

### Legal standing
✅ **Clean** — the Company and its subsidiary, directors and commissioners are **not party to any legal dispute**.

## 3a. SHAREHOLDER COMPOSITION ANALYSIS

| Bloc | Pre-IPO | Post-IPO (pre-option) | Post-option (final) | Read |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| GTP (Hartono) | 30.00% | 25.48% | **51.00%** | Becomes controller |
| BMSI (Bach) | 61.55% | 52.28% | **26.77%** | Sells control, stays ~27% |
| Bach founders (5) | 8.45% | 7.18% | 7.18% | Management continuity |
| Public | 0% | 15.06% | 15.06% | New float |

**Reading [OPINI]:** the cap table is, in effect, a **negotiated control transfer with a public float bolted on**. The public's 15.06% sits beneath a 51% Hartono controller and a 26.77% Bach legacy holder — a classic concentrated post-IPO register where minority influence is minimal. The investable question isn't "who controls" (clearly Hartono) but "will the controller run BACH for all shareholders or as a captive vendor to its tower empire."

## 5a. FUND-USAGE COMPOSITION & "% OF FUNDS TO DEBT"

| Price | Gross proceeds | **% to debt repayment** | % to genset (growth) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Rp400 (floor) | Rp246.0bn | **37.0%** | 63.0% |
| Rp500 (ceiling) | Rp307.5bn | **29.6%** | 70.4% |

*Debt repayment (Permata) is a fixed Rp91.02bn, so the % to debt **falls** as the price rises (the genset/working-capital slice absorbs the upside). **[OPINI]** Even at the floor, the majority (63%) is growth capital — a materially more investor-friendly use than a deleveraging IPO. Separately, the BMSI→GTP control sale (~Rp417–521bn) does not touch IPO proceeds.*

## 6a. DER ANALYSIS (as requested)

**Reported DER recomputed and confirmed** (total liabilities ÷ total equity): **0.83 → 0.88 → 1.30** — a sharp climb, driven by the debt-funded rental-fleet build-out (Rp369.7bn capex; CFF +Rp206bn in 2025).

**Post-IPO pro-forma (estimate):** the equity raise (~Rp246–307.5bn) plus the Rp91bn revolver repayment cuts DER to **~0.72–0.77× (≈0.75×)**. Direction is a clear de-lever — but BACH will likely **re-lever to keep buying gensets**, so the improvement may be temporary.

> **Honesty note:** this is the prospectus's *total-liabilities* DER. A clean interest-bearing-debt DER needs the bank-debt note (Permata + SMBC balances at 31 Dec 2025), which I did not fully extract — so the pro-forma is a labelled estimate, not a precise figure. *(See open questions.)*

---

## 🚩 RED-FLAG SUMMARY (for the deck)

1. **Concurrent control transfer to the Hartono family (GTP→51%) with NO mandatory tender offer** — structured via the "GTP already controller" resolution (8 Jan 2026); minorities get no control-premium exit.
2. **The control sale (BMSI→GTP, ~Rp417–521bn) is larger than the IPO raise** — the deal is primarily a control monetization; the public float is a sidecar, and the IPO book sets the Bach family's exit price.
3. **Related-party concentration** — #1 customer Protelindo (Hartono/TOWR) at 14–40% of revenue becomes a related party post-control; chair Anita Anwar sits on both sides.
4. **Debt-funded growth, thin cash** — DER 1.30×; only Rp11.97bn cash vs Rp595bn current liabilities; Rp369.7bn fleet capex outran CFO.
5. **Supplier dependency (stated #1 risk)** — reliance on Chinese genset suppliers; FX and supply-chain exposure.
6. **Cyclical growth** — 2025 surge is genset-rental tied to the data-centre/tower capex cycle; reversible.
7. **Pre-IPO dividend Rp129.1bn (2023)** = ~3.6× that year's net profit.
8. **Thin 15.06% float** + concentrated register → low liquidity, minimal minority influence.
9. **Governance thinness** — only 1 independent commissioner; conflicted chair; no ESA/ESOP alignment.

**Counterweights (be fair):** **cheap** (~10–13× P/E for ~100% profit growth, 29% ROE); **Hartono family** as controller brings capital strength, strategic backing and **captive demand** (TOWR/Protelindo/data centres); **growth-oriented** use of proceeds (~70% to revenue-generating assets); **positive, growing operating cash flow** (CFO Rp132bn); **clean legal record**; **structural demand tailwind** (Indonesia data-centre/telecom/electrification build-out).

---

## ❓ OPEN QUESTIONS / TO VERIFY BEFORE PUBLISHING
1. **Option exercise (strike) price** — set after bookbuilding; is it at, above, or below the IPO price? This determines BMSI's realised exit value and the MTO-fairness optics.
2. **Basis for GTP's pre-existing "controller" status at 30%** — board control? shareholders' agreement? OJK confirmation of the no-MTO position?
3. **Forward related-party framework with TOWR/Protelindo** — transfer-pricing governance, independent-party review, caps.
4. **Exact interest-bearing debt at 31 Dec 2025** (Permata + SMBC) — for a clean financial DER and net-debt/EV.
5. **Sustainability of genset-segment growth** — backlog, rental-fleet utilisation, data-centre pipeline; how much of 2025 was one-off.
6. **Issuance cost %** — confirm in the final prospectus (we state **gross, excluding cost**).
7. **POJK 25/2017 lock-up tension** — does the Feb-2026 cash subscription (Zulfahmi Fithri, at par) fall inside the 6-month pre-registration window? If so, an 8-month lock could apply despite the prospectus's blanket "no one acquired below price" statement.
8. **Customer-contract durability** — terms/renewal of the Protelindo MSA (6 May 2025) and SUPR work.

forensicMd:
## Thesis

PT Nitrasanata Dharma Tbk (JECX) brings to market the operator of JEC – Jakarta Eye Center, Indonesia's most established eye-care specialist: five specialty eye hospitals and 11 eye clinics across Jadetabek, Central Java, East Java, Bali, and Sulawesi, with a brand dating to 1984 and JCI accreditation (four consecutive periods) at one hospital. The core franchise is a genuine asset. But this is structured as a deleveraging and liquidity-event IPO, not a growth-capital raise. The defining feature is that public money substantially backfills a pre-IPO extraction: at every price in the Rp1,200–1,400 range, the majority of what the company itself raises repays bank debt, and the single largest debt line — Rp100bn to HSBC — repays a dividend-recap loan signed roughly 10 weeks before listing. Layered on top are a near-total founder-doctor cash-out in the secondary tranche, a 2024 profit collapse only partially recovered, covenant breaches at two subsidiaries, and a valuation in the low-60s P/E. The opportunity is a strong brand resetting its cost of capital; the question is how much of the raise actually reaches the business.

## How the business works

JEC operates an integrated eye-care network spanning the full continuum of care — from routine clinics through to tertiary specialty hospitals — letting it triage and escalate patients within its own system rather than referring them out. Revenue is multi-stream (self-pay, private insurance, and BPJS), which is precisely why the auditor named revenue recognition the Key Audit Matter for FY2025. Demand sits on a structural tailwind the company foregrounds: a 284.4m population that is aging and increasingly diabetic (20.4m diabetics in 2024, projected ~28.6m by 2050), which drives eye-care volume. The economics split cleanly between a healthy, cash-generative core (the parent plus clinics JECMI and hospital JMP) and a set of newer expansion vehicles that are the loss-making drag. (The revenue mix by service line is shown separately.)

## The offering & ownership

The deal offers 15.00% of post-IPO capital, but the structure matters more than the headline. Only 10% is primary (new shares, cash to the company); the other 5% is a secondary divestment by selling shareholder DR. Dr. Waldensius Girsang, whose stake falls from 6.216% to 0.594% — a roughly 97% exit. One-third of the gross offering value therefore flows to a single exiting individual rather than into the business. Free float lands at 14.657% true public (15.00% including ESA), sitting at the regulatory floor, which the prospectus itself flags as a liquidity risk.

Ownership carries an unusual control/economic mismatch. The largest single holder is listed competitor PT Sarana Meditama Metropolitan Tbk (SAME / OMNI Hospitals) at 28% pre / 25.2% post — held since at least April 2022, so a long-standing strategic rather than a pre-IPO flip — yet SAME does not control. Control vests in PT Magna Selaras Lestari (MSL, ~9–10%), with Dr. Darwan Madja Purba as ultimate beneficial owner; Darwan's effective control of roughly 21% (his direct 11.79% plus MSL's 9.13%) still sits below SAME's 25.2%. Whether a shareholders' agreement or standstill explains this is an open question.

Lock-up is weaker than the structure implies. No POJK 25/2017 eight-month lock-up is triggered, because the prospectus asserts no party acquired shares below the offer price in the six months before registration. The only contractual restriction is MSL's controller undertaking to hold for 12 months — covering only ~9.1%. The remainder, roughly 76% of pre-IPO shares, is freely transferable from day one against a 14.7% float: SAME's 25.2%, Darwan's 11.8%, the two Istiantoro heirs' combined 17.8%, Waldensius's residual, and the fragmented doctor tail. The ESA covers 11,165,000 shares (2.29% of the offering, 0.343% post-IPO) for 111 employees, excluding directors and commissioners, priced at the offer price as a fixed allotment. It is effectively a company-funded grant — acquisition cost and final tax borne by the company — vesting over four years at a maximum 25%/year, KPI-gated (Good 60% / Outstanding 80% / Exceptional 100% of each annual tranche; Poor/Fair forfeit), with unvested shares forfeit on resignation. There is no MESOP/ESOP; note that one passage mislabels the scheme "Employee Stock Option" while every mechanic describes an allocation.

## Governance & related parties

The board (Akta No.57, 9 Mar 2026) is chaired by the UBO himself, Dr. Darwan Madja Purba, as President Commissioner, alongside the founding Istiantoro family representative Agus Hermawan Istiantoro and an OMNI-aligned presence. Two of six commissioners are independent (33%), just above the 30% minimum — adequate but not a margin of safety. Independent Commissioner Heri Sunaryadi carries a capital-markets pedigree with a historical link to lead underwriter Trimegah, worth noting for independence perception though not a disqualifier. The two largest non-controlling individual holders, Rayyan and Nabbil Wardhana Istiantoro (8.897% each), inherited their stakes in 2024 from the late Dr. Vira Wardhana via will, hold no operating role, and carry no lock-up — a potential overhang. On related-party matters, the BCA and HSBC early repayments are stated to be non-affiliated transactions, while the NSB equity injection is an affiliated parent-to-subsidiary transaction handled under POJK 17/2020; the NSB land lessor is PT Hotel Indonesia Natour, a non-affiliated SOE. The legal record is clean: neither the company nor its directors and commissioners are party to any litigation — a genuine positive.

## Reading the financials

The trailing numbers tell a deceleration-and-shock story. Revenue grew +13.2% (2023), +7.6% (2024), then just +4.4% (2025). More striking, net profit collapsed roughly 51% in 2024 — from Rp127,284m to Rp62,472m — as G&A leapt from Rp326bn to Rp425bn (+30%) against +7.6% revenue, driven by expansion start-up drag at the 2024 openings (RS JEC Orbita Makassar, KUM JEC Pasuruan, KUM JEC Kendari; +Rp31.5bn), a new sustainability-cost accounting policy (+Rp20.3bn), and higher borrowing cost (+Rp12.4bn). Operating margin was crushed from 21.7% to 12.8%. The 2025 recovery is real but partial: net profit of Rp73,760m attributable to parent remains about 35% below the 2023 peak of Rp114,075m. The recovery narrative is not yet in the trailing P&L.

Two balance-sheet mechanics interlock. First, the current ratio fell to 0.69x — confirmed by the liabilities note: at 31 Dec 2025 long-term bank debt is nil, because the entire Rp260,654m of BCA term debt (parent Rp49.2bn, Orbita Rp149.1bn, JCS Rp62.3bn) sits in current liabilities. Orbita and JCS breached covenants at year-end, making the facilities callable and forcing reclassification; the BCA waiver is dated 11 March 2026, after the balance-sheet date, so it cannot reverse the year-end classification. Orbita's breach is the more severe — DER 3.14x, ICR 0.90x (EBITDA does not cover interest), DSCR 0.44 — alongside JCS (DSCR 0.49, ICR 1.84). Second, equity is flat to declining (Rp830,634m → Rp811,734m → Rp810,995m) despite positive earnings, because of aggressive pre-IPO dividends: Rp55bn (2023), Rp70.2bn (2024), Rp48.1bn (2025), with the 2024 payout exceeding that year's net profit — a >100% cash payout. The deleveraging plan rests on the IPO proceeds repaying roughly Rp275bn of bank debt across the parent and the two breaching subsidiaries. Importantly, the headline reported DER (0.79x at 2025) overstates financial leverage by about 2x, because the prospectus's "DER" uses total liabilities, dominated by payables and lease/dividend obligations; interest-bearing financial leverage is a moderate ~0.33x (bank debt Rp267.4bn ÷ equity), or ~0.40x including PSAK-116 leases of Rp58.2bn. The HSBC recap loan (Rp192bn drawn ~April 2026) lifts gross financial debt to roughly Rp432bn by 30 June 2026 before the IPO pays it back down — leverage rising on the eve of listing to fund a dividend, then a genuine, sizeable de-lever.

## Valuation

The ask is rich. On FY2025 attributable earnings against post-IPO shares (EPS Rp22.67), the trailing P/E is 52.9x at Rp1,200 and 61.8x at Rp1,400; even measured against the 2023 peak (EPS Rp35.06) it is 34.2x–39.9x, with P/B at roughly 3.3x–3.6x. That multiple capitalizes a recovery-and-expansion payoff the trailing P&L does not yet show, on decelerating revenue and a thin float. The sharpest valuation cross-check is internal to the cap table: SAME, the 25% non-controlling holder, is itself listed (OMNI Hospitals), so JEC's IPO multiple can be benchmarked directly against SAME's live trading multiple, plus MIKA, HEAL, and SILO. If OMNI/SAME trades materially cheaper than JEC's ask, that relative-value gap is the most honest read on the pricing. (Multiples are tabulated separately.)

## Bottom line

JECX is a strong, accredited eye-care franchise with a healthy cash-generative core, a clean legal record, and a real demand tailwind — wrapped in an offering whose cash flows point largely backward rather than forward. The majority of company proceeds repays debt (up to 70.4% at the floor), Rp100bn of it backfilling a dividend-recap loan, while a third of the headline deal cashes out an exiting founder-doctor; profit sits 35% below its 2023 peak, two subsidiaries breached covenants, and only ~9% of shares are locked against a floor-level float priced at 53–62x trailing earnings. The deleveraging plan is credible and, if executed, resets the cost of capital — but the trailing numbers and the use of proceeds ask investors to pay a premium for a recovery they cannot yet see, and to fund an extraction that has largely already happened.

narrativeMd:
# KUPAS PROSPEKTUS — JECX (PT Nitrasanata Dharma Tbk / "JEC – Jakarta Eye Center")

> Source: Prospektus Awal (preliminary), dated 22 June 2026, 504 pp. Lead underwriter: PT Trimegah Sekuritas Indonesia Tbk (full commitment). Status: registration submitted to OJK, **not yet effective**. All figures cross-checked against the prospectus; analytical opinions are explicitly labelled **[OPINI]**.

---

## 0. SNAPSHOT

| Item | Detail |
|---|---|
| Issuer | PT Nitrasanata Dharma Tbk (brand: **JEC – Jakarta Eye Center**) |
| Ticker | **JECX** (intended) |
| Sector | Private hospitals & clinics — **eye-care specialist** (KBLI 86101 / 86105) |
| Business | 5 specialty eye hospitals + 11 eye clinics across Jadetabek, C. Java, E. Java, Bali, Sulawesi |
| Founded | 1 Feb 1984 (as Klinik Mata Jakarta) → rebranded JEC 1993; legal entity 16 Dec 1993 |
| Nominal value | Rp16/share (post 1:50,000 stock split executed pre-IPO) |
| Price range | **Rp1,200 – Rp1,400** |
| Shares offered | **487,983,500 (15.00% of post-IPO capital)** = 325,322,300 new (10%) **+** 162,661,200 divestment (5%) |
| Gross offering value | **Rp585.6bn – Rp683.2bn** (excl. issuance cost) |
| To the **company** (primary) | Rp390.4bn – Rp455.5bn |
| To the **selling shareholder** (secondary) | Rp195.2bn – Rp227.7bn |
| ESA | 11,165,000 shares (2.29% of offering / 0.343% of post-IPO); 111 employees; no MESOP/ESOP |
| Post-IPO shares | 3,253,222,300 |
| Post-IPO market cap | **≈ Rp3.90tn – Rp4.55tn** |
| Indicative timeline | Bookbuilding 22–24 Jun; effective 29 Jun; offering 1–3 Jul; **listing 7 Jul 2026** |
| Controller / UBO | **PT Magna Selaras Lestari (controller)** / **Dr. Darwan Madja Purba** (ultimate beneficial owner) |
| Largest single holder | **PT Sarana Meditama Metropolitan Tbk (SAME / OMNI Hospitals) — 28% pre / 25.2% post** |

---

## 1. CURRENT SHAREHOLDERS

**Pre-IPO issued capital:** 2,927,900,000 shares (Rp46,846,400,000) out of authorized 5,000,000,000 (Rp80bn).

### Pre-IPO cap table (principal holders)
| # | Shareholder | Shares | % |
|---|---|---:|---:|
| 1 | **PT Sarana Meditama Metropolitan Tbk** (SAME / OMNI — *listed*) | 819,800,000 | **28.000%** |
| 2 | Dr. Darwan Madja Purba, Sp.M(K) | 383,700,000 | 13.105% |
| 3 | **PT Magna Selaras Lestari (MSL)** — *designated controller* | 297,155,097 | 10.149% |
| 4 | Rayyan Farrel Wardhana Istiantoro | 289,450,000 | 9.886% |
| 5 | Nabbil Wardhana Istiantoro | 289,450,000 | 9.886% |
| 6 | **DR. Dr. Waldensius Girsang, Sp.M(K)** — *the selling shareholder* | 182,000,000 | 6.216% |
| 7 | Prof. DR. Dr. Widya Artini Wijogo, Sp.M | 108,200,000 | 3.695% |
| — | Long tail of ~40+ individual doctors (ophthalmologists, anesthetists, etc.) | balance | ~19% |

### Post-IPO cap table
| Shareholder | Shares | % |
|---|---:|---:|
| PT Sarana Meditama Metropolitan Tbk (SAME) | 819,800,000 | 25.200% |
| Dr. Darwan Madja Purba | 383,700,000 | 11.794% |
| PT Magna Selaras Lestari (controller) | 297,155,097 | 9.134% |
| Rayyan Farrel Wardhana Istiantoro | 289,450,000 | 8.897% |
| Nabbil Wardhana Istiantoro | 289,450,000 | 8.897% |
| **DR. Dr. Waldensius Girsang** | **19,338,800** | **0.594%** |
| Public (Masyarakat) | 476,818,500 | 14.657% |
| ESA (employees) | 11,165,000 | 0.343% |
| **Total** | **3,253,222,300** | **100%** |

**Things you must not miss:**
- **The largest economic owner does NOT control.** SAME (OMNI Hospitals, a listed competitor/strategic) holds the single biggest block (28%→25.2%), yet control is vested in **MSL (only ~9–10%)** with **Dr. Darwan Madja Purba** as UBO. Darwan's effective control ≈ his direct 11.79% + MSL 9.13% ≈ **~21%**, still below SAME's 25.2%. **[OPINI]** This is an unusual control/ownership mismatch; a 25% strategic that is structurally non-controlling is a governance variable worth probing (is there a shareholders' agreement / standstill? Could OMNI move to control post-IPO?).
- **Heirs hold ~17.8% with zero lock-up.** Rayyan & Nabbil Wardhana Istiantoro (8.897% each) **inherited** their stakes from the late **Dr. Vira Wardhana, Sp.M(K)** (d. 2024) via will (Akta Wasiat No.115, 8 May 2024). Two young heirs, no operational role disclosed, no contractual lock-up → potential overhang.
- **The selling shareholder is exiting almost entirely** (see §3).

---

## 2. AFFILIATION WITH THE EXISTING STOCK MARKET

| Channel | Detail | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| **SAME (IDX: SAME)** | PT Sarana Meditama Metropolitan Tbk — OMNI Hospitals operator — owns 28%→25.2% of JECX. Held since **≥ April 2022** (Akta No.10/2022), i.e. a long-standing strategic, not a pre-IPO flip. | A **listed strategic** is JECX's largest holder. You can cross-read SAME's public financials, and **benchmark JECX's IPO multiple directly against SAME's live trading multiple** as a same-promoter sanity check. |
| Underwriter | PT Trimegah Sekuritas Indonesia Tbk (full commitment). | — |
| Board overlap w/ capital markets | Independent Commissioner **Heri Sunaryadi** has a capital-markets pedigree (ex-Trimegah / DPLK / BPJS-TK background). | **[OPINI]** Not a disqualifier, but note the historical Trimegah link for independence-perception purposes. |
| Group listed-entity exposure | JECX itself becomes the listed vehicle; subsidiaries (NSB, Orbita, JCS, JMP, JECMI) remain private. | — |

**No other cross-listings or affiliated IDX issuers identified** beyond SAME.

---

## 3. PUBLIC OFFERING & PROCEEDS — *the headline red flag*

### Structure
- **15% offered = 10% primary (new) + 5% secondary (divestment).**
- Primary (325,322,300 new shares) → **cash to the company.**
- Secondary (162,661,200 shares of **Dr. Waldensius Girsang**) → **cash to him personally, not the company.**

> 🚩 **RED FLAG — one-third of the headline deal is cash-out.** Of the gross offering value, **Rp195–228bn (33.3%) goes to a single exiting individual**, not into the business. Waldensius drops from **6.216% → 0.594% (a ~97% exit of his stake)**. A near-total founder-doctor exit concurrent with the IPO is a credibility/alignment question you should foreground in the deck.

### Proceeds table (excl. issuance cost; gross = price × shares)
| Price | Total offering | **To company** (primary) | **To seller** (secondary) | Post-IPO mkt cap |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Rp1,200 (low) | Rp585,580,200,000 | Rp390,386,760,000 | Rp195,193,440,000 | Rp3.90tn |
| Rp1,300 (mid) | Rp634,378,550,000 | Rp422,919,000,000 | Rp211,459,560,000 | Rp4.23tn |
| Rp1,400 (high) | Rp683,176,900,000 | Rp455,451,220,000 | Rp227,725,680,000 | Rp4.55tn |

### Free float
- **15.00% incl. ESA** / **14.657% true public.** This sits **at the regulatory floor**. **[OPINI]** Thin float = the prospectus's own admitted liquidity risk; expect higher post-listing volatility and easier price management.

---

## 4. LOCK-UP, ESA / ESOP

### Lock-up — *weaker than it looks*
- 🚩 **No POJK 25/2017 8-month lock-up applies to ANY shareholder.** The prospectus asserts **no party acquired shares below the offer price in the 6 months before registration**, so the mandatory 8-month restriction is **not triggered**.
- The **only contractual lock-up** is the controller's: **PT Magna Selaras Lestari** undertakes to retain control and **not transfer its shares for 12 months** after effectiveness (POJK 25/2017 controller obligation). That covers **only ~9.1%**.
- **Implication:** ~76% of pre-IPO shares — **SAME (25.2%), Darwan (11.8%), the two Istiantoro heirs (17.8% combined), Waldensius's residual, and the entire doctor tail** — are **freely transferable from day one of listing.** **[OPINI]** This is a large, legally-unrestricted supply overhang against a 14.7% float; arguably the single most important technical risk for early price action.

### ESA (Employee Stock Allocation)
| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| Size | 11,165,000 shares = 2.29% of offered / 0.343% of post-IPO |
| Recipients | 111 employees; **excludes** Directors & Commissioners |
| Price | At the **Offer Price**, fixed allotment (penjatahan pasti) |
| Funding | **Acquisition cost & final tax borne by the Company**; 0.1% sales tax also borne by Company → effectively a **company-funded grant** |
| Vesting | **4-year staggered, max 25%/yr, KPI-gated** (Good 60% / Outstanding 80% / Exceptional 100% of the annual tranche; Poor/Fair = 0%) |
| Forfeiture | Unvested shares forfeit on resignation/early retirement; lock-up applies |

- **No MESOP / stock-option program exists.** Note a **drafting inconsistency**: one passage labels it "Employee Stock *Option*," but every mechanic describes an **allocation/grant**, not options. Flag, don't repeat the error.

---

## 5. USE OF IPO FUNDS — *deleveraging-heavy / partial subsidiary bailout*

**Only the primary (new-share) proceeds accrue to the company.** The amounts in items 1–3 are **fixed/approximate Rupiah** (constant regardless of price); only item 4 (working capital) flexes with the final price.

| # | Use | Amount | Nature |
|---|---|---:|---|
| 1 | Early repay **Bank BCA** principal (parent) | Rp40,000,000,000 | Deleverage |
| 2 | Early repay **Bank HSBC** principal (parent) — **a dividend-recap loan** | Rp100,000,000,000 | **Repay dividend-funded debt** |
| 3a | **Equity injection to NSB** (Bali) — of which **Rp43.6bn prepays 30-yr Sanur land rent**, Rp6.4bn salaries | Rp50,000,000,000 | Fund pre-revenue sub |
| 3b | **Loan to Orbita** (6% intra-group) → repay Orbita's **BCA** debt | Rp100,000,000,000 | Deleverage distressed sub |
| 3c | **Loan to JCS** (6% intra-group) → repay JCS's **BCA** debt | Rp35,000,000,000 | Deleverage distressed sub |
| 4 | Parent working capital (salaries/allowances), by 31 Dec 2027 | **Residual: ~Rp130bn at ceiling / ~Rp65bn at floor** | Opex |

*Fixed earmarks (items 1–3) = **Rp325bn**. Bank-debt repayment (1 + 2 + 3b + 3c) = **Rp275bn**. See the §3a fund-usage composition and §5a "% of funds to debt" below.*

> 🚩🚩 **RED FLAG (elevated) — the single largest debt line is a DIVIDEND-RECAP REPAYMENT.** The HSBC facility (plafond Rp200bn, drawn Rp192bn) was signed **22 April 2026 — ~10 weeks before listing** — with the stated purpose *"Rekapitalisasi dividen yang telah disepakati"* (recapitalize an agreed dividend), GCP capped at 50%. So **≥Rp100bn of that loan funded a pre-IPO shareholder dividend, and Rp100bn of IPO proceeds now repays it.** **[OPINI]** Net economic effect: **public IPO money backfills a debt-funded dividend declared on the eve of the IPO.** Combined with the Rp195–228bn secondary cash-out (§3) and Rp173bn of 2023–25 cash dividends, this is a large pre-IPO extraction. Note also the dividend covenant tension — BCA/lenders restrict dividends if covenants aren't met (Orbita/JCS *were* in breach), so the dividend was routed through a *new* lender (HSBC) at the parent level.

> 🚩 **RED FLAG — ~Rp275bn of company proceeds (60.4% at the Rp1,400 ceiling, **70.4% at the Rp1,200 floor**) is bank-debt repayment**, across the parent and two **covenant-breaching subsidiaries (Orbita, JCS — see §6)**. **[OPINI]** Substantially **balance-sheet repair**, not growth capex. Adding the Rp50bn NSB injection (which itself mostly prepays Bali land rent for a *pre-revenue* clinic), **Rp325bn of the ~Rp390–455bn raised is pre-committed**, leaving only Rp65–130bn discretionary.

> ⚠️ The prospectus notes the BCA/HSBC early repayments are **not affiliated transactions**; the **NSB equity injection IS an affiliated transaction** (parent→subsidiary) handled per POJK 17/2020. Confirm the NSB **land lessor's identity** (rent is a recurring use of proceeds) is non-affiliated.

---

## 6. CURRENT COMPANY PERFORMANCE

### Income statement (Rp million)
| | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Revenue | 825,085 | 887,715 | 926,763 |
| Revenue growth | +13.2% | +7.6% | **+4.4%** ↓ |
| Gross profit | 515,504 | 554,540 | 576,689 |
| **Operating profit** | **178,750** | **113,468** | 126,312 |
| Pre-tax profit | 169,650 | 92,942 | 102,293 |
| **Net profit (total)** | **127,284** | **62,472** | 72,495 |
| Net profit attrib. to parent | 114,075 | 65,010 | 73,760 |
| EPS attrib. (Rp, full) | 38.96 | 22.22 | 25.24 |
| EBITDA | 265,097 | 222,870 | 237,621 |
| Net margin | 15.4% | 7.0% | 7.8% |

> 🚩 **RED FLAG — profit collapsed ~51% in 2024 and has only partially recovered.** Drivers (per MD&A):
> 1. **Expansion start-up drag** — RS JEC Orbita @ Makassar, KUM JEC @ Pasuruan, KUM JEC @ Kendari opened 2024 → **+Rp31.5bn** non-COGS expense.
> 2. **New "sustainability cost" accounting policy** adopted 2024 → **+Rp20.3bn**.
> 3. **Higher borrowing cost +Rp12.4bn** from Orbita's expansion loans.
> G&A leapt from **Rp326bn (2023) → Rp425bn (2024), +30%**, against +7.6% revenue → operating margin crushed from 21.7% to 12.8%. 2025 G&A stayed flat while gross profit rose, giving a partial recovery. **[OPINI]** 2025 net profit (Rp73.8bn) is **still 35% below the 2023 peak (Rp114bn)** — the "recovery" narrative is not yet in the trailing numbers.

## 3a. SHAREHOLDER COMPOSITION ANALYSIS

Composition by economic bloc (validated to the cap tables; pre and post sum to 100%):

**Reading [OPINI]:** Three structural facts jump out. (1) The **single largest owner (SAME, a listed strategic) does not control** — control sits with a ~21% founder bloc, an unusual split that should be probed for any shareholders' agreement. (2) **~38% of the company (heirs 17.8% + other doctors 20.5%) is held by parties with no contractual lock-up and no operating role** — a fragmented, freely-sellable base against a 14.7% float. (3) The selling shareholder's **near-total exit** is the clearest alignment red flag. Only MSL's ~9.1% is locked (12 months).

## 5a. FUND-USAGE COMPOSITION & "% OF FUNDS TO DEBT"

Bank-debt repayment from IPO = **Rp275bn** (BCA parent 40 + HSBC 100 + Orbita 100 + JCS 35). Because items 1–3 are fixed, the % to debt **rises as the price falls**:

| Price | Company (primary) proceeds | **% to bank-debt repayment** | % to subsidiaries (185) | % to discretionary WC | % of *total* offering to debt |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Rp1,200 (floor) | Rp390.39bn | **70.4%** | 47.4% | 16.7% | 47.0% |
| Rp1,300 (mid) | Rp422.92bn | **65.0%** | 43.7% | 23.1% | 43.3% |
| Rp1,400 (ceiling) | Rp455.45bn | **60.4%** | 40.6% | 28.6% | 40.3% |

*The "% of total offering" column divides by the full offering incl. the secondary tranche — shown only for completeness; the company never receives the secondary proceeds, so the **company-proceeds basis (60–70%) is the honest denominator**.*

> **[OPINI]** At every price point the **majority of what JEC itself raises goes to repaying debt** — and within that, the **Rp100bn HSBC line is a dividend-recap repayment**, not operating leverage. Growth-oriented use of IPO cash is, at best, the Rp65–130bn working-capital residual. This is a deleveraging/liquidity-event IPO, not a growth-capital IPO.

## 6a. DER ANALYSIS (as requested)

**Reported DER recomputed and confirmed** (total liabilities ÷ total equity): **0.63 (2023) → 0.75 (2024) → 0.79 (2025)** — rising. *But the prospectus's "DER" uses total liabilities*, which for JEC is dominated by trade payables, accruals, lease and dividend payables — **not** financial debt.

**Interest-bearing (financial) DER at 31 Dec 2025 — the figure that matters:**
- Bank debt Rp267.4bn ÷ equity Rp810,995m = **0.33×**
- Incl. PSAK-116 leases (Rp58.2bn): Rp325.6bn ÷ equity = **0.40×**

→ **The headline 0.79× overstates financial leverage by ~2×.** True financial leverage is moderate (~0.33–0.40×).

**Trajectory [estimates labelled]:** the **Rp192bn HSBC dividend-recap loan drawn ~April 2026** lifts gross financial debt to **~Rp432bn by 30 June 2026** (parent BCA ~42.9 + Orbita ~142.8 + JCS ~54.2 + HSBC 192), i.e. **leverage rises before the IPO to pay a dividend**. The IPO then repays **Rp275bn**, leaving residual bank debt ~Rp157bn (parent 2.9 + Orbita 42.8 + JCS 19.2 + HSBC 92). Net direction is a **genuine, sizeable de-lever**, but a precise post-IPO DER can't be stated honestly without the undisclosed **H1-2026 net profit and the exact 2026 dividend** — so I won't manufacture one. *(See §7 open questions.)*

---

## 🚩 RED-FLAG SUMMARY (for the deck)

1. **Dividend-recap backfill** — Rp100bn of IPO proceeds repays an HSBC loan (signed Apr 2026) raised to **fund a pre-IPO dividend**; public money reimburses a shareholder distribution.
2. **33% of the deal is a founder-doctor cashing out** (Waldensius 6.2%→0.6%, ~Rp195–228bn secondary).
3. **60–70% of company proceeds = debt repayment / subsidiary bailout** (70.4% at floor), not growth.
4. **Two subsidiaries (Orbita, JCS) breached bank covenants**; Orbita's EBITDA doesn't cover interest; waiver obtained only March 2026.
5. **Profit still 35% below 2023 peak**; revenue growth decelerating to +4.4%.
6. **Current ratio 0.69x — CONFIRMED** cause: Rp260.65bn breached BCA debt reclassified to current; long-term bank debt = nil.
7. **Pre-IPO dividends exceeded earnings in 2024** (>100% cash payout); equity flat for 3 years; cumulative 2023–26 payout >100% of earnings once the recap dividend is counted.
7. **Only ~9% locked up** (controller, 12mo); ~76% of pre-IPO shares freely sellable from day 1 vs a 14.7% float.
8. **~53–62x trailing P/E** — priced for a recovery not yet visible.
9. **Control/ownership mismatch** — largest holder (SAME 25%) is structurally non-controlling.

**Counterweights (be fair):** strong eye-care brand with JCI/KARS accreditation; healthy, cash-generative core (parent + JECMI + JMP); clean legal record; conservative *parent* balance sheet; structural demand tailwind; a credible deleveraging plan that, if executed, resets the cost of capital.

forensicMd:
## Thesis

INACO is a genuine 35-year-old consumer brand executing a *margin-led* profit turnaround while its top line shrinks for a second consecutive year — and it is asking the market to pay a demanding price for that story. PT Niramas Utama (JELI) is raising up to Rp392.0 bn in a 100%-primary offer (Rp900–Rp1,120 range), valuing the company at roughly 31–39× trailing parent earnings post-money. The bull case rests on a credible new export/OEM growth vector; the bear case is that 2025's profit surge rode a commodity tailwind, more than half the cash leaves the listed entity, and there is no hard lock-up on the 74% founder block. Strong brand, weak top line, rich price, several governance and structuring items to scrutinise.

## How the business works

JELI is an own-brand FMCG manufacturer built on coconut and natural-fibre desserts — nata de coco, mini jelly, pudding, aloe vera, ready-to-drink lines (I'm Coco, Jelly Drink) and gummies — sold under the INACO label ("Indonesia Nata De Coco"). The company *describes itself* as pioneer and market leader in the nata de coco category; note that the prospectus offers no quantified market-share data, so the "market leader" claim is the issuer's own characterisation rather than a verified fact (independent media support brand strength, not a confirmed #1 ranking). The economics combine four owned plants (~3.5 billion units/year of stated capacity), a 251-point national distribution network, exports to 30+ countries, and a meaningful contract-manufacturing (OEM/maklon) book — most notably toll-manufacturing for the gummy brand Yupi (contract expiring September 2026, a renewal to watch) and a new agreement signed 28 October 2025 under which subsidiary NPS will manufacture jelly products for Japan's Tarami via trading house Kawasho, with the machinery supplied by the Japanese side. That Tarami/Kawasho deal is the strategic anchor behind both the capacity expansion and the export thesis.

## The offering & ownership

This is a clean primary raise — 350,000,000 new shares (25.93% of post-IPO capital), no selling shareholders, no insider cash-out, underwritten on a full-commitment basis by sole lead PT Sucor Sekuritas. The crucial structural feature sits in the lock-up. Under POJK 25/2017 a lock-up only binds shareholders who acquired shares below the IPO price within six months before registration; the prospectus asserts no such party exists because the founder shares trace to a 2019 debt-to-equity conversion at par. The consequence is that there is **no statutory lock-up** on the 1,000,000,000 founder shares (74.07%). The only restraint is a voluntary 31 March 2026 pledge by UBO Ham Pak Japyusuf Hamdani not to relinquish *control* for at least 12 months — which, critically, is not a promise not to sell. He could sell down from 73.92% toward just above 50% and still honour the pledge. That gap between "registered float" and what can actually reach the tape is why the prospectus itself names share illiquidity as the primary investment risk.

## Governance & related parties

The board is tightly family- and insider-overlapped: five of the six NUI minority holders also sit on JELI's board, and NUI's own Komisaris Utama is family (Sadikun Wiratno). Minority protection rests on a single Independent Commissioner, Prof. Dr. Rycko Amelza Dahniel — factually a retired three-star police general (Komjen Pol) and former head of BNPT. This is legal and common in Indonesian IPOs, but a lone politically-exposed independent commissioner offers limited genuine minority-shareholder protection; it is worth noting neutrally for governance optics. On related parties, the controlling family has historically lent to the group; those loans (Rp21.25 bn at year-end 2025, due 2030) had their interest re-priced from ~1.0%/month to 0.58%/month effective 9 January 2026 — just before registration, a cosmetic positive that flatters reported 2025 finance costs and profit.

## Reading the financials

This is a textbook shrink-to-grow-margin story and it must be read carefully. Revenue *declined 4.49%* in 2025 to Rp753.1 bn — the second straight contraction (down 6.02% the year before) — yet net profit tripled to Rp39.0 bn. The driver was margin, not volume: management deliberately pruned low-margin SKUs while COGS fell faster on a cyclical raw-material (sugar/glucose/gelatin) price tailwind, supplier renegotiation, factory and labour efficiency, and end-2024 price increases. Disregard the "+54%" revenue figure some media reported — it is wrong; the audited numbers show a decline.

The signature red flag is cash. Despite tripling profit, 2025 operating cash flow *collapsed* from Rp123.5 bn to Rp20.5 bn (CFO/net profit of just 0.53×), as cash collected from customers fell roughly Rp70 bn below booked revenue. The receivables build is most plausibly a genuine seasonal timing effect — the pre-Ramadan 2026 peak was pulled forward into November–December 2025 and remained uncollected at year-end, with the bulk of receivables still not due and customers being blue-chip retail — but it depressed cash conversion heavily, and Q1-2026 normalisation is unconfirmed. Two further cautions: 2025 incorporates the SNU common-control acquisition (restated retrospectively), so the three-year series is not fully like-for-like; and the headline 26.8% ROE sits on a thin equity base that will roughly triple post-money, dropping prospective ROE toward 7–8%.

## Valuation

The reasoning here is straightforward and uncomfortable. On an absolute basis, ~31–39× trailing P/E is a demanding multiple to pay for a company whose top line has shrunk two years running and whose margin spike rides a reversible commodity tailwind. The bull case must assume the gummy/jelly plus Tarami export expansion reignites top-line growth *and* that 2025 margins are structural rather than cyclical; if margins normalise toward 2024 levels while revenue stays flat-to-down, the price looks expensive. A relative "premium vs. peers" judgement is deferred — that requires live IDX processed-food multiples as of publication, and several outlets have *characterised* the pricing as premium without an independently verified comparison. It is also worth weighing what the proceeds buy: over half the raise is injected into 99.99%-owned subsidiary NPS to fund capacity expansion (largely imported European machinery still "in negotiation"), meaning the public funds capex one level down, demand-contingent on the Tarami contract, with FX exposure on the equipment.

## Bottom line

JELI pairs a real, durable brand and a credible new export vector with a genuinely awkward financial profile: a shrinking top line, a margin surge that may be peak-cyclical, a 2025 cash-conversion break, and an aggressive price. The 100% primary structure and clean legal and audit standing are points in its favour, and the deleveraging from the raise is material. But the absence of a hard lock-up on 74% of shares, the down-streaming of most proceeds into a family-controlled subsidiary for not-yet-contracted machinery, and the demanding multiple on declining revenue leave little margin of safety. This is a brand worth respecting and a price worth questioning — one to underwrite only if you believe the export-led growth reignites and the 2025 margins hold.

This is the group's first listing, so there is no public-market track record. No PEP ties, no other listed-company affiliations, and no legal/reputational red flags found for the controller or the president commissioner.
