narrativeMd:
# IPO Forensic Analysis — PT Niramas Utama Tbk (Ticker: **JELI** / "Inaco")

> **Source document:** *Prospektus Awal* (Preliminary Prospectus), codename "Project Rocket – Draft Prospektus Awal v29", 392 pages, issued Jakarta 15 June 2026. Financials audited by KAP Paul Hadiwinata, Hidajat, Arsono, Retno, Palilingan & Rekan (PKF network), signing partner Saur Sitanggang CPA (AP No. 1393), opinion **unmodified (Wajar Tanpa Modifikasian)** with an **"Other Matter" (hal lain)** paragraph; report No. 01537/2.1133/AU.1/04/1393-1/1/VI/2026 dated 3 June 2026.
> **Analyst note:** This is a *preliminary* prospectus. Some figures (notably issuance-cost percentages and the final underwriter syndicate) are still blank ("•%") and the price is a *range*, not fixed. Treat all proceeds figures as "up to" the top of the range. Prepared for presentation use; opinions are clearly labelled as analysis, facts are attributed to the prospectus.

---

## 0. Executive Snapshot

| Item | Detail |
|---|---|
| Issuer | PT Niramas Utama Tbk ("Perseroan") — brand **INACO** |
| Ticker | **JELI** (IDX), sector Consumer Non-Cyclicals / Processed Food |
| Core business | Coconut/natural-fibre dessert F&B: nata de coco, mini jelly, pudding, RTD drinks (I'm Coco, Jelly Drink), gummies; OEM/contract manufacturing |
| Shares offered | **350,000,000 new shares** = **25.93%** of post-IPO capital (100% primary / new shares) |
| Price range | **Rp900 – Rp1,120** per share (par Rp100) |
| Gross proceeds | **Rp315.0 bn (low)** – **Rp392.0 bn (high)** — headline "Rp392 bn" assumes the *top* price |
| Net proceeds | **Not quantifiable from this draft** — equals gross *less* total *biaya emisi* (issuance costs), which are undisclosed (all fee lines "•%"). No cost estimate applied. See §3.1 |
| Post-IPO market cap | **Rp1,215 bn – Rp1,512 bn** (1.35 bn shares) |
| Sole lead underwriter | **PT Sucor Sekuritas** — *full commitment*; no affiliation with issuer |
| Controlling shareholder (PSP) | PT Niramas Utama International (NUI), 99.80% → **73.92%** post-IPO |
| Ultimate Beneficial Owner | **Ham Pak Japyusuf Hamdani** (a.k.a. "Yusuf Hamdani") |
| Indicative timeline | Bookbuilding 15–22 Jun 2026 · OJK effective 29 Jun · Public offer 1–3 Jul · Allotment 3 Jul · Listing **7 Jul 2026** |
| Lock-up | **No statutory POJK 25/2017 lock-up applies** (see §4 — this is a red flag) |
| ESOP / ESA / MESOP | **None** |
| Dividend policy | Up to **30% payout**; company has **never paid a dividend** |
| 2025 revenue / net profit | Rp753.1 bn (**−4.5% YoY**) / Rp39.0 bn (**+235.5% YoY**) |

**One-line thesis:** A 35-year-old, genuine market-leader brand (INACO) executing a *margin-led* profit turnaround while **revenue shrinks for a second consecutive year**, priced at a **premium multiple (≈31–39× trailing P/E post-money)**, with **>50% of proceeds down-streamed into a subsidiary**, **no hard lock-up on founder shares**, and a **2025 cash-conversion collapse** that needs explaining. Strong brand, weak top line, rich price, several governance/structuring items to scrutinise.

---

## 1. Current Shareholders & Ownership Chain

### 1.1 Pre-IPO cap table (per Akta No. 25 dated 20 Feb 2026; par Rp100)

| Shareholder | Shares | Nominal (Rp) | % |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| PT Niramas Utama International (NUI) | 998,000,000 | 99,800,000,000 | **99.80%** |
| Sadikun Wiratno | 2,000,000 | 200,000,000 | 0.20% |
| **Total issued & paid-up** | **1,000,000,000** | **100,000,000,000** | **100.00%** |

Authorised capital (Modal Dasar) was raised from Rp100 bn to **Rp400 bn** (4,000,000,000 shares) in 2026 to accommodate the IPO.

### 1.2 Post-IPO cap table (pro forma, top of range)

| Shareholder | Shares | % | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| PT Niramas Utama International (NUI) | 998,000,000 | **73.92%** | Controlling shareholder (PSP) |
| Sadikun Wiratno | 2,000,000 | 0.15% | Komisaris Utama |
| **Public (Masyarakat)** | 350,000,000 | **25.93%** | Free float |
| **Total** | **1,350,000,000** | **100.00%** | All 1.35 bn shares will be listed |

### 1.3 Ownership chain & Ultimate Beneficial Owner (UBO)

```
Ham Pak Japyusuf Hamdani  (declared UBO / PSP, also Director Utama of JELI & President Director of NUI)
        │  (controls)
        ▼
PT Supra Pangan Lestari ──89.90%──┐
Tony Loesiahari ──────────2.50%──┤
Tjokro Susilo ────────────2.50%──┤
Erijanto Djajasudarma ────2.50%──┼──►  PT Niramas Utama International (NUI)  [holding co., KBLI 64200]
Adhi Siswaya Lukman ──────1.35%──┤
Sadikun Wiratno ──────────1.25%──┘
        │  (99.80% of JELI)
        ▼
PT Niramas Utama Tbk (JELI)  ──►  subsidiaries: NPS 99.99%, SNU 83.21%, KDU 99.86%
```

- The UBO was **formally designated** as Ham Pak Japyusuf Hamdani in Feb 2026 to comply with **POJK 45/2024** — a pre-IPO housekeeping step. (BO declaration to AHU filed 26 Feb 2026.)
- **Insider overlap:** Five of the six NUI minority shareholders (Tony Loesiahari, Tjokro Susilo, Erijanto Djajasudarma, Adhi Siswaya Lukman, Sadikun Wiratno) **also sit on JELI's board** as commissioners/directors. NUI's Komisaris Utama is **Sonny Hamdani** (family). Several bios nonetheless state "no affiliation with the PSP" — technically referring to the narrow legal definition of *Afiliasi*; in substance this is a **tightly family/insider-held structure**.
- **NUI legacy name:** NUI was founded in 1983 as "PT Gloria Cipta Agung" — a long-standing family vehicle, not a newly minted SPV.

### 1.4 Board (post-Akta No. 26, 14 Aug 2025)

**Dewan Komisaris:** Sadikun Wiratno (Komut) · Tjokro Susilo (Komisaris) · **Prof. Dr. H. Rycko Amelza Dahniel, M.Si.** (Komisaris Independen).
**Direksi:** Ham Pak Japyusuf Hamdani (Dirut) · Adhi Siswaya Lukman · Erijanto Djajasudarma · Trisno Kuntjoro Wirawan · Tony Loesiahari · Philip Hamdani.

> ⚠️ **Governance flag — politically-exposed Independent Commissioner.** The lone Independent Commissioner, **Rycko Amelza Dahniel**, is a *retired three-star police general* (Komjen Pol). Verified externally: former **Head of BNPT** (National Counter-Terrorism Agency, 2023), ex-Kalemdiklat Polri, ex-Kabaintelkam, ex-Kapolda Sumut & Jateng, and a former aide to President SBY. This is legal and common in Indonesian IPOs, but a single PEP independent commissioner offers limited genuine minority-shareholder protection. Worth noting neutrally for governance optics.

---

## 2. Affiliations with the Existing Stock Market & Related Parties

### 2.1 Affiliation with capital-market players
- **Underwriter independence:** PT Sucor Sekuritas (and all supporting professionals) **declare no affiliation** with the issuer (per Bab XII–XIII). Underwriting is **full commitment**.
- **No existing listed affiliate** identified. NUI, Supra Pangan Lestari, and the subsidiaries (NPS, SNU, KDU) are all private. JELI would be the family group's **first listed vehicle**. (No cross-holding with other IDX issuers disclosed.)

### 2.2 Subsidiaries (Perusahaan Anak)

| Sub | Full name | Business | Ownership | Revenue contrib. | Status | Note |
|---|---|---|---:|---:|---|---|
| **NPS** | PT Niramas Pandaan Sejahtera | F&B manufacturing (Pasuruan) | 99.99% | 5.98% | Active | **Recipient of 51% of IPO proceeds** |
| **SNU** | PT Supra Natami Utama | Coconut cooking products (Sukabumi) | 83.21% | 6.05% | Active | **Acquired 2025** (common-control) |
| **KDU** | PT Karunia Distribusi Utama | F&B trading (Surabaya) | 99.86% | 0% | **Inactive** | Dormant shell |

### 2.3 Related-party loans (controllers lending TO the group)

| Lender (related party) | Amount (31 Dec 2025) |
|---|---:|
| Philip Hamdani | Rp13,950,000,000 |
| Yusuf Hamdani (UBO) | Rp7,300,000,000 |
| **Total** | **Rp21,250,000,000** |

- Long-term, due **2030**. Interest **reduced from 1.0%/month (≈12.7% eff.) to 0.58%/month (≈7.2%/yr) effective 9 Jan 2026** — i.e., re-priced to a more market-like rate **just before** the registration. Separate facilities also exist to NPS (Yusuf Hamdani, plafond Rp10 bn) and SNU (Yusuf & Philip Hamdani, plafond Rp15 bn each), all at 0.58%/month.
- **Read-through:** the group has historically been part-funded by the controlling family at above-market rates; the pre-IPO rate cut is a cosmetic-positive that improves reported finance costs and 2025 net profit.

### 2.4 Affiliated *makloon* (toll-manufacturing) arrangement
- JELI ↔ NPS toll-manufacturing agreement (No. 001/GA/NU/2017, amended Apr 2025): NPS produces/packs finished food products for JELI. Intra-group; eliminated on consolidation but relevant to how the 51% capex injection flows.

### 2.5 Material *third-party* commercial agreements (NOT affiliated — growth-relevant)
- **Tarami Corporation + Kawasho Foods Corporation (Japan), 28 Oct 2025:** NPS will **manufacture & supply jelly products to Tarami via Kawasho**; Tarami/Kawasho supply the machinery & equipment. 3-year term from 1 Mar 2025. **JELI guarantees NPS's payment & performance obligations.** → This is the **strategic anchor** behind the gummy/jelly capacity expansion and the export thesis. (Tarami is a major Japanese konjac-jelly maker; Kawasho is a trading house — a credible OEM/export relationship.)
- **PT Yupi Indo Jelly Gum, 6 Sep 2019 (amended 6 Sep 2024):** JELI contract-manufactures finished products for Yupi (the large gummy brand). **Expires 5 Sep 2026** → renewal risk to monitor.
- **PT Gihon Karya Persada, 30 Jun 2025:** construction contract Rp4.003 bn (70% complete at YE2025).

---

## 3. Public Offered Shares, Proceeds & Red Flags

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Shares offered | 350,000,000 (all **new/primary**) |
| % of post-IPO capital | 25.93% |
| Price range | Rp900 – Rp1,120 |
| **Gross proceeds** | **Rp315.0 bn → Rp392.0 bn** |
| Issuance costs | Disclosed as **"•%"** (management/underwriting/professional fees still blank in preliminary prospectus) |
| Greenshoe / over-allotment | None disclosed |
| Selling shareholders | None (no secondary sell-down at IPO) |

### 3.1 Proceeds & Float Box — gross (disclosed), net (not quantifiable), float (disclosed)

**What the prospectus actually gives vs. what it does not.** Gross proceeds and public float are fully determinable from the offer terms. **Net proceeds are NOT** — the document leaves them blank: the *biaya emisi* section reads "•%" on every fee line (underwriting / management / selling / accountant / legal / notary / BAE / listing / printing), and the **Tabel Proforma Ekuitas** (Bab IX) carries `[●]` in both its offer-price header (`Rp[●]/saham`) and its **Biaya Emisi** column. So the proforma equity table *exists* but is **un-populated**; do not present it as if it contains figures.

**The only hard cost figure in the document** is **Note 15 — Beban Tangguhan (Deferred Charges) Rp300,000,000**, explicitly *"professional fees deferred… in connection with the plan to conduct an IPO."* That is **only the accountant/legal/notary work capitalized through 31 Dec 2025** (~0.08% of gross) — it is **not** total issuance cost and excludes the underwriting fee, which is the dominant, deal-size-contingent component crystallized only at bookbuilding. Treat Rp300 mn as a *partial lower bound on the professional-services slice*, nothing more.

**Gross proceeds & public float (disclosed):**

| | At Rp900 (floor) | At Rp1,120 (cap) |
|---|---:|---:|
| Shares offered (100% primary) | 350,000,000 | 350,000,000 |
| **Gross proceeds = float value** | **Rp315.0 bn** | **Rp392.0 bn** |
| Public free float (registered) | **25.93%** | **25.93%** |

> Because the issue is 100% new shares, the public's subscription *is* the proceeds — so the **rupiah float value equals gross proceeds** at any given price. Don't double-count them as separate magnitudes.

**Net proceeds — not estimated.** Net proceeds = gross proceeds **less total *biaya emisi*** (all issuance costs: underwriting/management/selling, professional, BAE, OJK/BEI/KSEI, printing, roadshow, etc.). Because **none of these are quantified in the preliminary prospectus** (every fee line reads "•%" and the proforma-equity *Biaya Emisi* column is "[●]"), **we do not put a number on net proceeds or apply any cost assumption.** Net will simply be **gross minus the full, currently-undisclosed cost stack**, finalised only in the **final prospectus** after bookbuilding. (The one disclosed cost data point — Note 15's Rp300 mn deferred professional fees, above — is a partial component only and is *not* a basis for estimating the total.)

**Float nuance worth a line in the explainer (ties to the lock-up flag, §4):** the *registered* free float is 25.93%, but with **no statutory lock-up** the founder block (74.07%) is legally unrestricted, so **day-1 *tradable* float is technically up to 100%**; realistic active float ≈ the 25.93% public slice — which is precisely why the prospectus names **illiquidity as the primary investment risk**. The gap between "registered float" and "what can actually reach the tape" is the sharp teaching point. (Free float of 25.93% comfortably clears the typical IDX minimum of ~15–20% depending on post-IPO equity tier; confirm the exact tier in the final listing approval.)

### Red flags / structuring notes
1. **"Up to Rp392 bn" is the high-end headline.** At the floor price (Rp900) the company raises only **Rp315 bn** — a ~Rp77 bn (20%) swing that directly affects how much of the use-of-proceeds plan is funded. Several media outlets quoted "Rp392 bn" without the caveat.
2. **100% primary** — no insider cash-out at IPO. Positive (alignment), but see §4: the lack of a hard lock-up means sell-down can happen *post-listing*.
3. **No greenshoe / price-stabilisation** mechanism disclosed → first-day price discovery is unbuffered; the prospectus itself flags **share illiquidity** as the *primary investment risk*.
4. **Media data error (fact-check):** *IDNFinancials* reported "2025 sales … an increase of 54.18% from IDR 488.43 bn." **This is wrong.** The audited prospectus shows 2024 revenue of **Rp788.4 bn** and 2025 revenue **declining 4.49%** to Rp753.1 bn. *DealStreetAsia* and *TrenAsia* correctly report a revenue *decline*. Do **not** repeat the "+54%" figure.

---

## 4. Lock-up, ESOP & ESA

### 4.1 Lock-up — **the single most important deal-structure red flag**
- The prospectus states that under **POJK 25/2017**, a lock-up (8 months post-effective) only binds parties who acquired shares **below the IPO price within the 6 months before registration**. It then asserts **no such shareholder exists**, because the founder shares trace to a **2019** debt-to-equity conversion (NUI converted receivables into 500m shares at par Rp100) — well outside the 6-month window.
- **Consequence:** there is **no statutory share lock-up**. All **1,000,000,000 founder shares (74.07%)** are, in principle, **freely tradable from listing day**.
- The only restraint is a **voluntary statement (31 Mar 2026)** by UBO Ham Pak Japyusuf Hamdani that he **will not relinquish *control*** for **≥12 months**.
  - ⚠️ **Critical nuance:** "not relinquishing control" ≠ "not selling shares." He could sell down from 73.92% toward just above 50% **and still satisfy the pledge**. This is materially weaker than a true founder share lock-up, and the prospectus's own "Risiko Fluktuasi Harga Saham" explicitly warns that significant insider selling could pressure the price.
- **Verification done:** The 2019 dating of the conversion is consistent with the cap table being static 2019→2025 (no change 2024–2025; only the 2026 authorised-capital increase). The lock-up exemption claim therefore appears *technically correct* under POJK 25/2017 — but the *economic* effect (no hard lock on 74% of shares) is the takeaway. **Confirm the deed dates in the legal opinion (Bab XVII) before publishing.**

### 4.2 ESOP / ESA / MESOP
- **None.** No employee stock option, employee share allocation, or management option program exists in this offering (verified by full-text search; only standard *Penjatahan Pasti* / *Penjatahan Terpusat* allotment mechanics are present).

---

## 5. Use of IPO Proceeds & Red Flags

Net proceeds (after issuance costs) are allocated as:

| # | Allocation | % | ~Rp at top end | Type |
|---|---|---:|---:|---|
| 1 | **Equity injection into subsidiary NPS** → NPS buys gummy/jelly production machinery | **51.04%** | ~Rp200 bn | Capex (Level II, via sub) |
| 2 | JELI capex — machines, warehouse capacity, logistics | **18.36%** | ~Rp72 bn | Capex (Level I) |
| 3 | **Partial repayment of Bank Mandiri KMK 1 & KMK 2** (Rp94 bn → Rp54 bn, i.e. **repay ~Rp40 bn**) | **10.63%** | ~Rp42 bn | Deleveraging |
| 4 | Working capital (raw materials, opex, marketing) | **19.97%** | ~Rp78 bn | Working capital |

**Machinery detail (Level II / NPS):** key items are a **Tanis Confectionery BV (Netherlands)** gummy line (~Rp106 bn, EUR, ~55% of NPS capex) and an **Extrufood B.V. (Netherlands)** extruder (~Rp61 bn, EUR), both via local representative **PT Teknologi Prima Utama** (declared *not affiliated*), plus a reach-truck from PT Denko Wahana Sakti (not affiliated). Total NPS capex budget ≈ **Rp191.6 bn**; JELI Level-I capex budget ≈ **Rp69.0 bn**.

### Red flags / scrutiny points
1. **>50% of proceeds leave the listed entity and go into a 99.99%-owned subsidiary (NPS).** Minority/public shareholders own JELI, not NPS directly. The structure is legitimate (NPS is the manufacturing arm; the 0.01% held by Philip Hamdani becomes ~0% after injection, i.e. NPS becomes ~100% JELI-owned), **but** public investors should understand they are funding capex *one level down*, governed by JELI's board (which is family-controlled). Monitor that the equity injection (planned Q3 2026) and the machine purchases — **"still in negotiation"** per the prospectus — actually execute and at the budgeted prices (FX risk: EUR/CNY/USD exposure on imported machines).
2. **Capacity expansion is demand-contingent on the Tarami/Kawasho OEM deal.** If that 3-year contract underdelivers or is not renewed, the new gummy/jelly capacity could be under-utilised → fixed-cost drag (the prospectus's own risk factor).
3. **FX timing risk:** machine prices are quoted in EUR with offer validity dates in late 2025/early 2026 ("s/d 31-01-2026", "s/d 06-11-2025"). If procurement slips and Rupiah weakens, the Rp budget may overrun.
4. **Debt repayment is sensible** (cuts the most expensive short-term Mandiri working-capital lines) but is only 10.6% of proceeds; leverage remains meaningful post-IPO (see §6).
5. **No proceeds earmarked for the Hamdani related-party loans** — those remain on the books to 2030 (neutral, but note the family keeps an interest-earning claim on the company post-IPO).

---

## 6. Current Company Performance (Forensic Financial Review)

All figures Rp, consolidated, audited (FY ending 31 Dec). 2025 figures incorporate the **SNU acquisition (common-control business combination)** — see comparability caveat below.

### 6.1 Income statement

| Line | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 | 2024→25 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **Revenue** | 838,941 | 788,429 | **753,051** | **−4.5%** |
| COGS | (592,680) | (528,823) | (462,274) | −12.6% |
| **Gross profit** | 246,261 | 259,606 | **290,777** | +12.0% |
| Gross margin | 29.4% | 32.9% | **38.6%** | +5.7pp |
| Selling exp. | (159,983) | (165,140) | (169,804) | +2.8% |
| G&A exp. | (51,834) | (56,816) | (54,785) | −3.6% |
| Finance cost | (23,470) | (19,798) | (15,644) | −21.0% |
| **Pre-tax profit** | 6,608 | 20,241 | **51,699** | +155.4% |
| **Net profit (total/consolidated)** | 1,680 | 11,632 | **39,025** | **+235.5%** |
| — of which: attributable to **parent** | 2,395 | 12,916 | 39,023 | — |
| — of which: non-controlling interest (NCI) | (715) | (1,284) | 2 | — |
| Net margin (on total) | 0.20% | 1.48% | **5.18%** | +3.7pp |
| **EPS (basic & diluted)** | Rp2.40 | Rp12.92 | **Rp39.02** | — |
*(Rp million unless per-share. Weighted-avg shares 1,000,000,000 all years.)*

> **Read carefully — parent vs. total earnings.** EPS (Note 36) is computed on **profit attributable to owners of the parent** ÷ 1.0 bn shares, **not** on consolidated total net profit. The two diverge in 2023–24 because **the subsidiaries (NPS, and pre-acquisition SNU) were loss-making and NCI absorbed those losses** — so *owner*-attributable profit was *higher* than consolidated total (2023: Rp2.40 bn parent vs Rp1.68 bn total). This converged by 2025 (NCI ≈ nil). Implication: the headline "+235% total net profit" overstates the *owner-level* earnings trajectory somewhat (parent profit grew +439% then +202%, vs total +593% then +236%), and the 2023 base is even thinner than the consolidated figure suggests. Use **parent profit (Rp39.02 bn, 2025)** for per-share/valuation work.

**What actually happened (per MD&A):** Revenue fell because management **deliberately pruned low-margin SKUs** ("produk yang kurang profitable") as a strategic consolidation. COGS fell faster, driven by (a) **lower raw-material market prices in 2025** (sugar/glucose/gelatin), (b) intense supplier renegotiation, (c) dropping unprofitable products, and (d) factory efficiency; they also **raised selling prices from end-2024**. Finance costs fell on renegotiated interest rates. The profit surge is therefore **margin- and cost-led, not volume-led.**

> 🔍 **Earnings-quality challenge:** This is a textbook "shrink-to-grow-margin" story. The gross-margin jump of **+5.7pp in a single year** rests heavily on a **cyclical raw-material (sugar) price tailwind** that can reverse, plus price increases that could pressure volume further. The headline "+235% net profit" should **not** be read as durable organic growth — the top line has now contracted **two years running** (≈−5% CAGR 2023→2025).

### 6.2 Cash flow — the standout red flag

| | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Net profit | 1,680 | 11,632 | 39,025 |
| **Operating cash flow (CFO)** | 16,143 | **123,549** | **20,527** |
| **CFO / Net profit** | 9.6× | 10.6× | **0.53×** |
| Cash from customers | 810,531 | 807,402 | **682,656** |
| Investing CF | (29,578) | (13,407) | (22,836) |
| Financing CF | 31,649 | (46,543) | (7,299) |
| Net Δ cash | 18,214 | 63,599 | (9,607) |
*(Rp million.)*

> 🚩 **The 2025 profit jumped 3.4× while operating cash flow COLLAPSED 83% (Rp123.5 bn → Rp20.5 bn).** Cash collected from customers (Rp682.7 bn) fell ~Rp70 bn **below** booked revenue (Rp753.1 bn) — exactly matching the receivables build-up. In 2025, JELI earned reported profit it did not convert to cash. CFO/NI of **0.53×** is the metric to put on the slide.

### 6.3 Receivables — investigate, but the explanation is plausible

| | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Trade receivables (net) | 123,258 | 104,213 | **174,132** |
| YoY change | — | −15% | **+67%** |
| **DSO (days)** | 53.6 | 48.2 | **84.4** |

- Receivables **+67% while revenue fell** — a classic warning sign. **However**, management's explanation is credible: the **Ramadan/Idul Fitri 2026 sales peak was pulled forward into Nov–Dec 2025** (Ramadan began ~Feb 2026), and those sales were still uncollected at year-end.
- **Aging supports this:** of Rp179.0 bn gross, **Rp154.9 bn (86.5%) was "not yet due"**; >120-days past-due was only Rp4.86 bn (impairment allowance Rp4.85 bn). Customers are **blue-chip retail** (Lotte Mart, Indomarco/Indomaret, Lion Super Indo, Pulau Baru Mandiri) plus USD export receivables (RR Imports, India). No single dominant customer (largest named is ~Rp2.1 bn).
- **Verdict:** likely a **genuine seasonal timing effect, not channel-stuffing** — but it depressed 2025 cash flow heavily. **Key open question:** did Q1 2026 collections normalise CFO back up? The prospectus carries some 31-Mar-2026 data points; request/await interim cash-flow confirmation before treating the cash gap as fully benign.

### 6.4 Balance sheet & leverage

| | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Total assets | 514,046 | 522,597 | 552,114 |
| Cash & equivalents | 41,551 | 105,150 | 95,543 |
| Trade receivables | 123,258 | 104,213 | 174,132 |
| Inventory | 108,807 | 103,466 | 90,946 |
| Total liabilities | 407,366 | 404,078 | 406,594 |
| **Total equity** | 106,680 | 118,519 | **145,520** |
| **Liabilities / Assets** | 0.79 | 0.77 | **0.74** |
| ROE | 1.6% | 9.8% | **26.8%** |
| ROA | 0.3% | 2.2% | **7.1%** |

**Interest-bearing debt (2025):** short-term bank Rp119.3 bn + current portion LT bank Rp23.2 bn + LT bank Rp25.7 bn = **~Rp168.2 bn bank debt**; + related-party loans Rp21.3 bn; + lease liabilities Rp19.3 bn. Cash ~Rp95.5 bn → **net debt ≈ Rp113 bn (incl. leases)**. Net-debt/equity ≈ **0.6–0.8×**. **Highly working-capital-intensive:** in 2025 the company drew Rp535 bn and repaid Rp517 bn of short-term bank lines (revolver churn).

> **Equity-quality note:** *Tambahan modal disetor* (additional paid-in capital) swung from +Rp5.0 bn (2024) to **−Rp2.62 bn (2025)**, and prior "proforma restructuring equity" lines disappeared — both consequences of the **2025 SNU acquisition booked as a common-control combination (PSAK 38 / "PSAK 338" rev. 2025)**, restated retrospectively. This **reduces clean comparability** of the 3-year series; the 2025 numbers are not a like-for-like continuation of 2023–24. Flag this when presenting the trend.

### 6.5 ROE caveat
26.8% ROE looks excellent, but it sits on a **thin Rp145.5 bn equity base** and is flattered by (i) the cyclical margin spike and (ii) high financial leverage. Post-IPO, equity roughly **triples** (≈Rp510–517 bn post-money) and the same profit will produce a **much lower ROE (~7–8%)** — the headline ROE will *not* persist after the raise. Don't anchor on 26.8%.

## 7. Additional Useful Information for the Presentation

### 7.1 Business profile & competitive position
- **Founded 1990; 35+ years.** **Self-described** as pioneer and **"market leader"** in nata de coco / coconut-fibre dessert F&B. ⚠️ **Attribution caution (UU ITE):** the prospectus provides **no quantified market-share data** to support the "market leader" label — it is the issuer's own characterisation. Independent media describe INACO as "one of the best-known" nata de coco brands, which supports *brand strength* but not a verified #1 position. Cite it as the company's claim, not as fact. Brand **INACO** = "Indonesia Nata De Coco."
- **Product lines:** Nata de Coco (multiple flavours), Mini Jelly, Pudding, Aloe Vera, RTD "I'm Coco", Jelly Drink, Mini Gummies/confectionery, Snack "Mini Bites", "EnerGel" drink.
- **4 plants:** Bekasi (HQ + main), Pontianak (Aloe vera), Pandaan (Industry-4.0 plant + East-Indonesia distribution hub), Sukabumi (nata de coco; via SNU).
- **Estimated capacity ≈ 3.5 billion units (pcs)/year** across all lines.
- **Distribution:** **251 distribution points** nationwide; target ~300 distributors within 3 years; micro-distribution via two-wheeler fleet for traditional retail.
- **Exports to 30+ countries** (incl. Japan, India, China, Thailand, Australia, Canada, USA; priority markets India, Saudi Arabia, Philippines; 2028 push into Africa & Europe).
- **Certifications:** HACCP (2022), ISO 22000:2018 (2026); SAP S/4HANA ERP since 2017.
- **Workforce (group, 31 Dec):** **550 (2025)** vs 567 (2024) vs **596 (2023)** — headcount *declining*, corroborating the efficiency/SKU-rationalisation story. By entity (2025): Perseroan 445, NPS 71, SNU 34, **KDU 0** (dormant; was 16 in 2023). Labour is **~61% contract** at the parent (272 contract vs 173 permanent); "Crew" headcount fell 220→172 over the period — i.e. the margin gains are partly *labour-cost* driven, not only raw-material prices.
- **Capacity utilization: NOT disclosed.** The prospectus quantifies capacity (~3.5 bn pcs/yr) but **never discloses the current utilization rate**, even though 51% of proceeds funds capacity *expansion*. ⚠️ This is a meaningful disclosure gap: if existing lines are under-utilised, adding capacity is hard to justify; the omission should be raised directly with management/IR.

### 7.2 Industry context
- Indonesia F&B contributes ~**7.13% of GDP** (Kemenperin, cited in prospectus) — a resilient, large base. Health-food / coconut-based megatrend (millennial/Gen-Z) is the company's stated tailwind.
- **IPO-market context:** JELI is only the **2nd IDX IPO of 2026** (after BSA Logistics/WBSA, listed 10 Apr 2026) — an unusually thin new-issue market, which can cut both ways for aftermarket demand.

### 7.3 Underwriter
- **PT Sucor Sekuritas** (CEO Bernardus Wijaya) — an active small/mid-cap IPO underwriter on IDX. Sole lead, **full commitment**. (Track record is mixed across its IPO book; full commitment means Sucor absorbs unsold shares, which supports deal completion but says nothing about aftermarket performance.)

### 7.4 Auditor & opinion
- **KAP Paul Hadiwinata, Hidajat, Arsono, Retno, Palilingan & Rekan** (PKF International member). **Unmodified opinion** for FY2025/24/23. The **"Other Matter" (Hal Lain) paragraph** has now been read in full (Bab XVIII) and is **benign**: it (i) restricts the report's use to the IPO prospectus, and (ii) discloses that these financials are a **reissuance** — the same FY were previously reported on **2 Mar 2026** and **6 Apr 2026**, then reissued with "several changes and additional disclosures in connection with the proposed IPO." **Not** a going-concern qualification. *Forensic note:* the twice-reissued comparatives (consistent with the SNU common-control restatement) are why the 3-year series is not fully like-for-like — see §6.4. Signing partner Saur Sitanggang CPA (AP No. 1393), report dated 3 Jun 2026.

### 7.5 Legal & litigation
- Legal opinion confirms the company, its directors/commissioners, and subsidiaries face **no pending claims/somasi, no civil/criminal/administrative/tax/bankruptcy proceedings**. **Clean legal standing.**

### 7.6 Dividend policy
- Intends to pay **up to 30% of net profit** annually (after legal reserve), subject to RUPS. **Has never paid a dividend** to date. (Given the heavy capex plan, near-term cash dividends are unlikely to be material.)

### 7.7 Key risk factors (per prospectus)
1. **Primary risk: raw-material availability & price volatility** — especially **sugar**, which requires government import quotas/permits (raw-sugar import mechanism). A 2025 *tailwind* that is equally a forward *risk*.
2. **Share illiquidity** — explicitly named on the cover as the primary *investment* risk (small free float, single underwriter, no greenshoe).
3. **PSP control risk** (73.92% post-IPO) — interests may diverge from public shareholders.
4. **Subsidiary-performance risk** (proceeds concentrated in NPS).
5. **Export/foreign-regulation risk**; macro & FX risk on imported machinery/raw materials.
6. **Price-below-book risk** — prospectus warns the offer price exceeds net book value per share (dilution of intrinsic value if price corrects).

> 💰 **Valuation verdict (analysis):** On an **absolute** basis, **~31–39× trailing P/E** is a demanding multiple for a company with a **declining top line** and a **margin spike riding a commodity (sugar) tailwind**. The bull case must assume the gummy/jelly + Tarami export expansion **reignites top-line growth** and that 2025 margins are **structural, not cyclical**; if margins normalise toward 2024 levels and revenue stays flat-to-down, the multiple looks expensive. **Relative verdict deferred:** a peer comparison requires **live** P/E/EV-EBITDA for comparable IDX processed-food names *as of the publication date* — pull these yourself before asserting "premium vs. peers," since multiples move and any stale figure would be the easiest number for a reader to discredit. ## 10. Bull vs. Bear (for the slide)

**Bull case**
- 35-yr category-leading brand (INACO) with real moats: distribution (251 points, 30+ export countries), brand recall, R&D/certifications.
- Credible new growth vector: **gummy/jelly capacity + Japanese OEM (Tarami/Kawasho) export deal** funded by the raise.
- Demonstrated **margin & efficiency turnaround**; deleveraging via IPO; resilient staples category.
- 100% primary raise (no insider cash-out at IPO); clean legal/audit standing.

**Bear case**
- **Top line shrinking**; profit growth is **cost/commodity-driven and possibly peak-cyclical**.
- **Cash conversion broke down in 2025**; needs Q1-2026 confirmation.
- **No hard lock-up** on 74% of shares; **demanding ~31–39× trailing P/E** (high in absolute terms); **>50% of cash leaves the listco** into a family-controlled subsidiary for not-yet-contracted machinery.
- Sugar-import dependency; thin independent governance.

forensicMd:
## Thesis

PT Esa Medika Mandiri (EMMI) is a >20-year independent distributor of medical, laboratory and pharmaceutical devices — carrying global principals including Medtronic, Medicon, Bowa, Penlon and Air Liquide — with two light-manufacturing factories (Cikupa, Solo). The defining fact is concentration: roughly **96% of revenue (96.1% in 2025) goes to the government**, and an even higher **98.5% of gross profit**. Recent growth has been explosive but lumpy and margin-dilutive, funded by a heavily-geared balance sheet. The IPO is 100% primary, raising mostly working capital. It prices at a demanding trailing P/E, and a pre-IPO land revaluation has cosmetically improved how the leverage reads. This is a higher-risk name than its headline growth implies.

## How the business works

EMMI is, in substance, a single operating company — the parent — with a cluster of near-zero subsidiaries (EBT, MMM, SUN, SSN, EWI) consolidated in 2023, contributing between 0.003% and 0.929% of revenue. It wins government and multilateral tenders, pre-funds the inventory and project goods, delivers, then carries long government receivables. There are **no long-term sales contracts** (a stated risk), so revenue is inherently lumpy. The 2024 surge was driven by specific named tenders rather than recurring demand: **IsDB (Islamic Development Bank) tenders of Rp47.4 bn** and a **World Bank SOPHI tender of Rp101.7 bn, of which only ~20% (~Rp20 bn) was recognised in 2025**. That leaves ~Rp81 bn of SOPHI revenue still to recognise — genuine near-term visibility — but beyond the current backlog, earnings depend entirely on winning fresh tenders, and a budget cut, lost tender or payment delay hits ~96% of the business.

## The offering & ownership

The company offers **522,857,000 new shares (30% of post-IPO capital, all primary)** at **Rp446–Rp515** (par Rp50), with no selling shareholders and no greenshoe. Underwriting is full-commitment by **PT BRI Danareksa Sekuritas (lead) and PT INA Sekuritas Indonesia** — a credibility step up, with both declaring no affiliation to the issuer. The "Ina" overlap with lender Bank Ina Perdana is coincidental: INA Sekuritas is the former Nikko Sekuritas Indonesia, unrelated to Salim-group Bank Ina Perdana, so there is no underwriter-lender conflict.

Critically, there is **no statutory POJK 25/2017 founder lock-up**: the prospectus states no party acquired shares below the IPO price in the six months before registration, so the current structure (set by Akta No. 153, 23 Feb 2026) is treated as predating the window. The only restraint is Surya Gunawan Widjaja's **12-month commitment to retain control (≥50%)** — which permits selling down toward 50%. The founder block is in principle freely tradable from listing day; this entire position hinges on the capital history genuinely predating the window and warrants verification. The **ESA** allots up to **52,285,700 shares (10% of offered / 3% post-IPO)**, including "Saham Penghargaan" reward shares carrying a notably long **30-month lock-up**.

## Governance & related parties

All owners are individuals — no holding company. Surya Gunawan Widjaja is the declared controller (UBO), and the Widjaja family holds 85% pre-IPO falling to 59% post-IPO. The governance item worth a careful read: the **Independent Commissioner is a former senior Ministry of Health (Kemenkes) medical-device regulator** — per the disclosed CV, Director of Medical Device & PKRT Assessment (2021–2024) and Director of Medical Device Supervision (2016–2021), now also leading a medical-device industry association. This is reproduced factually with no inference of wrongdoing. Analytically it cuts both ways: deep regulatory and tender expertise for a 96%-government supplier, against weakened substance of "independence" for a recent regulator of the exact products EMMI sells. Related-party items include **building rental income of Rp2.61 bn (2025)** and a **joint venture contributing equity-method profit of Rp4.10 bn (2025)** — roughly 12% of net profit, non-core income that flatters the bottom line and whose counterparty and durability merit confirmation.

## Reading the financials

The trajectory is striking but qualified. Sales grew **+122.5% in 2024 and +18.1% in 2025 to Rp454.64 bn**, but the 2024 surge came with a **gross-margin collapse from 51.3% to 35.3%** (35.9% in 2025) — this is low-margin government volume, not premium product. Leverage is the second story: **interest expense (Rp30.44 bn in 2025) consumed 247% of operating profit in 2023 and still 46% in 2025**, the signature of a debt-funded model. Note that 2025 net profit attributable to parent (**Rp34.13 bn**) exceeds total profit (Rp32.44 bn) because non-controlling interests absorbed Rp1.69 bn of subsidiary losses — EPS must be struck on parent profit.

Working capital is structurally stretched: the **current ratio was below 1.0 in every year (0.48× / 0.82× / 0.92×)**, with government receivable days lengthening to **110 in 2025**. The structural item to flag is the **Rp37.65 bn land revaluation** of the Serpong HQ in 2025 — independently appraised and proper under PSAK 16, but **non-cash**. It lifted equity from Rp73.4 bn to Rp145.0 bn and **cut reported liabilities/equity from 8.74× to 2.89×** right before the IPO. Strip it out and DER is ~3.91× — the deleveraging is partly cosmetic, since the cash debt did not fall; the equity denominator was revalued up. The FY2024/2023 comparatives were also **restated (*disajikan kembali*)**, superseding the earlier reports — the restatement reason should be read in the notes.

## Valuation

At the range, the post-IPO market cap is **Rp777.3 bn–Rp897.6 bn**, putting EMMI on a **~22.8×–26.3× trailing P/E** on 2025 parent profit. That is demanding for a ~96% government-concentrated, ~7% net-margin, heavily-leveraged, tender-dependent distributor — you are paying a growth multiple for earnings whose visibility past the SOPHI/IsDB backlog is low. Quality caveats compress the case: ~12% of net profit is JV equity income, book value (and therefore P/BV) is flattered by the land revaluation, and ROE drops to ~8.5% once the equity base roughly doubles post-raise. The land revaluation makes P/BV *look* cheaper; stripping it out, it is not. Natural live comparables are IDX-listed med-device names IRRA and OMED, whose current multiples should be pulled at publication date before any relative cheap/expensive judgement.

## Bottom line

EMMI offers real growth, a genuine sector tailwind (≈8.68% market CAGR to 2030, plus JKN and TKDN support), credible principals, better underwriting than recent small-cap peers, and ~Rp81 bn of SOPHI backlog for near-term visibility. Against that sits a concentrated and fragile core: ~96% government revenue with no recurring contracts, heavy leverage (interest at 46% of operating profit), chronic negative working capital, margin-dilutive growth, a book value and leverage profile flattered by a pre-IPO revaluation, no hard founder lock-up, and a demanding ~23–26× P/E. The structure is sensible for the working-capital engine it funds, but the margin of safety is thin — returns hinge on tender win-rate and collection discipline, and the risks run deeper than the headline growth suggests.

narrativeMd:
# IPO Forensic Analysis — PT Esa Medika Mandiri Tbk (Ticker: **EMMI**)

> **Source document:** *Prospektus Awal* (Preliminary Prospectus), 346 pages, issued 22 June 2026. FY2025 financials audited by **KAP Mirawati Sensi Idris (member of the Moore Global network)**, **unmodified opinion**, report dated 15 June 2026, signed Emanuel Handojo Pranadjaja CA, CPA (AP.0929). **FY2024/2023 comparatives were RESTATED (*disajikan kembali*)** — the pre-restatement reports (dated 3 Dec 2025 and 25 Jul 2025) were superseded. See §6 and §7.4.
> **Analyst note (method):** preliminary prospectus → price is a *range*, issuance-cost lines are "[*]%". Where I quantify net proceeds it is **back-solved from the prospectus's own allocation arithmetic** (caveated), not a heuristic. No stale peer multiples are used. Prepared for presentation use (Kang Ritel / kupas-prospektus); opinions are labelled as analysis and attributed to the prospectus, UU ITE-aware (documented facts only, no allegations).

---

## 0. Executive Snapshot

| Item | Detail |
|---|---|
| Issuer | PT Esa Medika Mandiri Tbk (HQ Esa 8 Building, Gading Serpong, Tangerang) — ~20+ yrs in the business |
| Ticker | **EMMI** (IDX), sector Healthcare — **medical-device / lab / pharma DISTRIBUTION + light manufacturing** |
| Core business | Independent distributor of medical/lab/pharma devices (principals incl. Medtronic, Medicon, Bowa, Penlon, Air Liquide) + 2 factories (Cikupa, Solo); **~96% of revenue is to GOVERNMENT** |
| Shares offered | **522,857,000 new shares** = **30%** of post-IPO capital (100% primary) |
| Price range | **Rp446 – Rp515** (par Rp50) |
| Gross proceeds | **Rp233.2 bn (floor) – Rp269.27 bn (cap)** |
| Implied net proceeds | **≈ Rp256.4 bn at cap** (back-solved → implied *biaya emisi* ≈ 4.8% of gross; see §3.1) |
| Post-IPO market cap | **Rp777.3 bn – Rp897.6 bn** (1,742,857,000 shares) |
| Underwriters | **PT BRI Danareksa Sekuritas (lead) + PT INA Sekuritas Indonesia** — *full commitment*. **Higher-tier than the Sucor deals (JELI/PRDL)** |
| Controlling shareholder | **Surya Gunawan Widjaja (30% → 21%)**; Widjaja family 85% → 59% post-IPO; Eddy Lie 15% → 10% |
| Ultimate Beneficial Owner | **Surya Gunawan Widjaja** (declared controller) — all shareholders are individuals, no holding company |
| Lock-up | **No statutory POJK 25/2017 founder lock-up applies** (prospectus claims no below-IPO-price acquisitions in the 6-mo window) — only a 12-month control commitment. **Weak, like JELI** |
| ESA | Yes — up to 52,285,700 shares (10% of offered / 3% post-IPO); reward shares carry a **30-month lock-up** |
| Subsidiaries | 5 (EBT, MMM, SUN, SSN, EWI) — **all tiny or dormant** (0.003–0.929% of revenue) |
| Dividend | See §7.4 (confirm policy/history in Bab XI) |
| Indicative timeline | Bookbuilding 22–24 Jun · OJK effective 30 Jun · Public offer 2–6 Jul · Listing **8 Jul 2026** |
| 2025 revenue / net profit | Rp454.6 bn (**+18.1% YoY**, after +122.5% in 2024) / **Rp34.13 bn** attrib. parent (+210% YoY) |

**One-line thesis:** A fast-growing but **~96% government-dependent**, **thin-margin (gross 36%)**, **heavily-leveraged (interest = 46% of operating profit)**, **working-capital-negative (current ratio <1 every year)** medical-device distributor whose recent growth came from **lumpy multilateral tenders (IsDB, World Bank SOPHI)** rather than recurring revenue. The IPO funds working capital (68.7%) and partial debt repayment. A **Rp37.65 bn land revaluation** (independently appraised, but timed for IPO) cut reported leverage from 8.74× to 2.89× and inflated book value. Priced at a **demanding ~23–26× trailing P/E**. Better underwriting (BRI Danareksa) and a real sector tailwind, but the government concentration + leverage + tender-lumpiness make this a higher-risk name than the headline growth suggests.

---

## 1. Current Shareholders & Ownership Chain

### 1.1 Pre- and post-IPO cap table (par Rp50)

| Shareholder | Pre-IPO shares | Pre % | Post-IPO % | Post-IPO+ESA % |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **Surya Gunawan Widjaja** (controller) | 367,246,000 | 30% | **21%** | 21% |
| Andrew Ignatius Widjaja | 285,410,000 | 23% | 16% | 16% |
| Florian Chris Widjaja | 195,200,000 | 16% | 11% | 11% |
| Andrian Matthew Widjaja | 195,200,000 | 16% | 11% | 11% |
| Eddy Lie | 176,944,000 | 15% | 10% | 10% |
| **Public** | — | — | **30%** | 27% |
| **ESA** | — | — | — | 3% |
| **Total** | **1,220,000,000** | 100% | 1,742,857,000 | 100% |

- **All natural-person owners — no holding company** (unlike JELI's NUI or PRDL's Prodia Utama). The **Widjaja family holds 85% pre-IPO / 59% post-IPO**; with Eddy Lie aligned, 69% post-IPO. **Surya Gunawan Widjaja** is the declared **controller (PSP)**.
- Authorised capital 4,300,000,000 shares (Rp215 bn). The current structure was set via Akta No. 153 (23 Feb 2026), two days before registration (25 Feb 2026) — relevant to the lock-up question (§4).

### 1.2 Board — governance flag worth a careful read
> ⚠️ **The Independent Commissioner is a former senior Ministry of Health medical-device regulator.** Per the disclosed CV, this commissioner (appointed 2026) previously served at the **Ministry of Health (Kemenkes)** as **Director of Medical Device & PKRT Assessment (2021–2024)**, Director of Medical Device Supervision (2016–2021), and acting director roles in pharmaceutical/medical-device resilience and public-drug governance (2020–2022) — and now also leads a medical-device industry association. **This is the documented prospectus disclosure; I draw no inference of wrongdoing.** Analytically it cuts two ways: (i) **bull** — deep regulatory expertise and government/tender access for a company selling ~96% to government; (ii) **governance optics** — a recent senior regulator of the exact products EMMI sells, seated as the *independent* commissioner shortly after leaving office, weakens the substance of "independence" and creates conflict-of-interest optics. A sophisticated reader should weigh this; present it factually, not accusatorially (UU ITE).

---

## 2. Affiliations with the Existing Stock Market & Related Parties

### 2.1 Capital-market affiliations
- **No listed-affiliate parent** (unlike PRDL↔PRDA). EMMI is independently family-held; this would be the family's first listed vehicle.
- **Underwriters: BRI Danareksa Sekuritas (lead) + INA Sekuritas Indonesia.** Both declare **no affiliation with the issuer** (Bab XIII–XIV). BRI Danareksa is the IB arm of state-bank BRI — a credibility step up from the Sucor small-cap deals.
- 🔎 **Fact-check (cleared):** EMMI repays debt to **Bank Ina Perdana**, and a co-underwriter is **PT INA Sekuritas**. The "Ina" name overlap is **coincidental** — **PT Ina Sekuritas is the former PT Nikko Sekuritas Indonesia** (an Indonesia–Japan JV since 1990, renamed 2023), **unrelated to Bank Ina Perdana** (Salim group). **There is no underwriter-lender conflict here.** (Flagged and dismissed so the wrong inference isn't drawn.)
- **Listed competitors named in the prospectus:** **PT Jayamas Medica Industri (OMED)** and **PT Itama Ranoraya (IRRA)** — the natural live comparable set for valuation (§8).

### 2.2 Related parties (per the financial statements)
- **Building rental income** (Rp2.61 bn in 2025) and a **joint venture** (*ventura bersama*) contributing **equity-method profit of Rp4.10 bn (2025)** / Rp1.63 bn (2024); JV carrying value Rp5.73 bn. The JV profit is ~12% of 2025 net profit — non-core, equity-method income that flatters the bottom line; identify the JV counterparty and its sustainability.
- Confirm the full related-party transactions note (Bab VIII / financial-statement notes) for any sales/purchases with Widjaja-family entities; the subsidiaries (below) were brought into the group in 2023 (a pre-IPO consolidation).

### 2.3 Subsidiaries — mostly tiny or dormant
| Sub | Business | Owned | Rev contribution 2025 | Status |
|---|---|---:|---:|---|
| EBT | Wholesale lab/pharma/medical devices | 60% | 0.003% | Operating (Solo factory) |
| MMM | General trading/construction/services | 55% | – | Operating |
| SUN | Retail+wholesale lab/pharma/medical devices | 90% | 0.929% | Operating (Cikupa factory) |
| SSN | Not yet operating | 99% | – | Dormant (est. 2024) |
| EWI (indirect via SSN) | Not yet operating | 55% | – | Dormant (est. 2024) |

> **Read-through:** the "Grup" is effectively **one operating company (the parent)** with a cluster of near-zero subsidiaries gathered in 2023. Consolidation adds little revenue but does introduce NCI (which absorbed Rp1.69 bn of losses in 2025 — see §6).

---

## 3. Public Offered Shares, Proceeds & Red Flags

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Shares offered | 522,857,000 (all **new/primary**) |
| % of post-IPO capital | 30.00% |
| Price range | Rp446 – Rp515 (par Rp50) |
| **Gross proceeds** | **Rp233.2 bn → Rp269.27 bn** |
| Selling shareholders | None |
| Greenshoe | None disclosed |
| Issuance-cost % | "[*]%" (blank) — net back-solvable, §3.1 |

### 3.1 Proceeds & Net (back-solved from the prospectus's own math)
Item 1 (debt) is a **fixed Rp50.0 bn**; items 2–3 are **11.8% + 68.7% = 80.5% of net** → debt = 19.5% of net → **net proceeds ≈ Rp50.0 bn ÷ 0.195 ≈ Rp256.4 bn** (at cap). Against gross Rp269.27 bn that implies **biaya emisi ≈ Rp12.9 bn ≈ 4.8% of gross** — reasonable for a deal this size (cheaper % than PRDL's tiny deal). *Caveat: assumes the percentages were computed at the cap-price scenario; at the floor (gross Rp233.2 bn) the fixed Rp50 bn debt repayment absorbs a larger share.*

### 3.2 Red flags / structuring notes
1. 🟠 **Working-capital-dominated use of proceeds (68.7%).** The bulk of the raise funds **inventory + project-related goods + receivables**, not productive assets — the signature of a thin-margin, project-based distributor. Returns depend on working-capital efficiency and continued tender wins; it does not build durable earning capacity the way a factory would.
2. 🟠 **~19.5% (Rp50 bn) repays bank debt** (Bank Ina Perdana, partial) — a partial deleveraging of a heavily-geared balance sheet; substantial debt remains (§6).
3. 🟡 **Capex (11.8%) builds the Cikupa factory** — modest; the company is primarily a distributor, not a manufacturer.
4. 🟡 **Mid-cap, but the price (Rp446–515) is a real number** (not gocap) — the cover still flags **share illiquidity** as the secondary risk; 30% free float at ~Rp0.78–0.90 tn market cap is investable but small.

---

## 4. Lock-up, ESOP & ESA

### 4.1 Lock-up — **weak (like JELI, not PRDL)**
- The prospectus invokes POJK 25/2017 but states **no party acquired shares below the IPO price within the 6 months before registration**, therefore **no statutory lock-up binds the founders.** The current shareholding (Akta No. 153, 23 Feb 2026) is treated as predating the window (a par re-split, not a fresh below-price issuance). **→ The 70%/85% founder block is, in principle, freely tradable from listing day.**
- The only restraint is **Surya Gunawan Widjaja's 12-month control commitment** (≥50%). As with JELI, "not relinquishing control" ≠ "not selling" — he can sell down toward 50% and still comply.
- ⚠️ **Verify in Bab VIII (capital history)** that the founder shares genuinely predate the 6-month window (par Rp50 vs IPO Rp446–515 — any recent at-par issuance *would* trigger the lock-up). The entire "no lock-up" position hinges on this, exactly as it did for JELI.

### 4.2 ESA
- Up to **52,285,700 shares (10% of offered / 3% post-IPO)**. Comprises fixed-allotment shares and **"Saham Penghargaan" (reward shares) with a 30-month lock-up** (notably long vs PRDL's 12 months) — better retention alignment. Confirm the split between employee-paid and company-funded (free) portions and quantify any cash/P&L cost.

## 5. Use of IPO Proceeds & Red Flags

| # | Allocation | Basis | ~Rp (at cap, net≈256.4 bn) | Type |
|---|---|---|---:|---|
| 1 | **Repay bank debt** — Bank Ina Perdana KMK/DL facility (repay Rp44.9 bn; total facility Rp90 bn, outstanding Rp87.8 bn → residual ~Rp42.9 bn) + a 2nd facility | **Fixed Rp50.0 bn** | Rp50.0 bn (~19.5%) | Deleveraging |
| 2 | Capex — **Cikupa factory building** | 11.8% of net | ~Rp30.3 bn | Capex |
| 3 | **Working capital** — project-related goods + raw materials/inventory | 68.7% of net | ~Rp176 bn | Working capital |

> The dominant use (working capital) confirms the business model: EMMI must pre-fund inventory and carry ~110-day government receivables to service tenders. The IPO essentially **recapitalises the working-capital engine** and trims interest cost — sensible for the model, but it means equity holders are funding the float, and returns hinge on win-rate and collection discipline.

## 6. Current Company Performance (Forensic Financial Review)

All figures Rp billion (audited, consolidated; FY2024/2023 **restated**).

### 6.1 Income statement — explosive but margin-dilutive, debt-heavy growth

| Line | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **Net sales** | 172.98 | 384.93 | 454.64 |
| YoY growth | −6.9% | **+122.5%** | +18.1% |
| Gross profit | 88.70 | 135.91 | 163.43 |
| **Gross margin** | **51.3%** | **35.3%** | **35.9%** |
| Operating profit | 9.15 | 40.60 | 66.84 |
| **Interest expense** | (22.63) | (29.51) | **(30.44)** |
| — interest / operating profit | 247% | 73% | **46%** |
| JV equity income | – | 1.63 | 4.10 |
| Pre-tax profit | 0.83 | 13.84 | 43.09 |
| **Net profit (total)** | 0.94 | 11.25 | 32.44 |
| **— attributable to parent** | 0.94 | 11.01 | **34.13** |
| — non-controlling interest | – | 0.25 | **(1.69)** |
| Net margin (on total) | 0.5% | 2.9% | 7.1% |
| **EPS (Rp, pre-IPO 1.22 bn sh)** | 0.77 | 9.02 | **27.98** |

> 🔍 **Three things to put on the slide.** (1) **Revenue +122.5% in 2024** is real but came with a **gross-margin collapse from 51% to 35%** — the growth is *low-margin government/project volume*, not premium product. (2) **Interest expense is enormous** — it ate 247% of operating profit in 2023, still **46% in 2025**; this is a debt-funded model. (3) **2025 net profit attributable to parent (Rp34.13 bn) exceeds total (Rp32.44 bn)** because a subsidiary's losses were absorbed by NCI (−Rp1.69 bn) — EPS is on **parent** profit; don't divide total profit by share count.

### 6.2 The concentration — ~96% GOVERNMENT, tender-driven and lumpy

| | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **Government segment — % of sales** | **86.9%** | **95.5%** | **96.1%** |
| Government segment — % of gross profit | 86.9% | 95.5% | **98.5%** |
| Non-government — % of sales | 13.1% | 4.5% | 3.9% |

> 🔴 **The single defining risk.** EMMI is, in substance, a **government medical-equipment supplier (~96% of sales, ~98.5% of gross profit in 2025)** — the prospectus's stated #1 risk. The recent growth was driven by **specific lumpy multilateral tenders**, not recurring demand: **IsDB (Islamic Development Bank) tenders** (Rp47.4 bn) and a **World Bank SOPHI tender (Rp101.7 bn, of which only ~20% / ~Rp20 bn was recognised in 2025)**. **Implications:** (i) there is **forward visibility** of ~Rp81 bn of SOPHI revenue still to be recognised (a genuine near-term positive); but (ii) beyond these named tenders, revenue depends entirely on **winning new government/multilateral tenders** — there are **no long-term sales contracts** (a stated risk), and a government budget cut, a lost tender, or a payment delay hits ~96% of the business. Earnings visibility past the current backlog is low.

### 6.3 Balance sheet — negative working capital + the pre-IPO revaluation

| | 2023 | 2024 | 2025 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Total assets | 340.63 | **714.39** | 564.44 |
| Current assets | 101.42 | 454.32 | 286.78 |
| Current liabilities | 212.87 | **555.43** | 312.23 |
| **Current ratio** | **0.48×** | **0.82×** | **0.92×** |
| Total liabilities | 284.57 | 641.02 | 419.42 |
| Total equity | 56.06 | 73.37 | **145.01** |
| **Liabilities / Equity** | 5.08× | **8.74×** | **2.89×** |
| Receivable days | – | 94 | **110** |
| ICR / DSCR | 1.06 / 1.26 | 1.47 / 1.56 | 2.47 / 1.94 |

> 🚩 **Two red flags.** (1) **Current ratio < 1 in every year (0.48 / 0.82 / 0.92)** — current liabilities *exceed* current assets throughout; the company chronically funds working capital with short-term liabilities. Combined with 110-day government receivables and the 2024 asset balloon (assets Rp714 bn) → contraction (Rp564 bn), this is a **structurally stretched, swing-prone balance sheet**. (2) **The Rp37.65 bn fixed-asset revaluation (land at the Serpong HQ) in 2025.** It is **independently appraised** (proper under PSAK 16 — not fabricated), **but** it is **non-cash**, it **inflated equity by ~26%** (Rp73.4 → 145.0 bn), and it **cut reported leverage from 8.74× to 2.89× right before the IPO** while boosting book value for P/BV. Strip it out and liabilities/equity is ~3.9× and P/BV is materially higher. **Treat the deleveraging as partly cosmetic** — the cash debt did not fall; the equity denominator was revalued up.

### 6.4 Audit & restatement
- **FY2025:** KAP **Mirawati Sensi Idris (Moore network)** — mid-tier; **unmodified** opinion.
- **FY2024/2023:** **restated (*disajikan kembali*)**; pre-restatement reports (3 Dec 2025, 25 Jul 2025) superseded. The restatement *reason* is not established from the summary — **read the restatement note** (a recurring diligence item across these 2026 IPOs: JELI, PRDL, and EMMI all restated comparatives pre-IPO). The earlier reports were co-signed by an auditor named **Juninho Widjaja (AP 1029)**; given the owners are the Widjaja family, **verify this is an unrelated individual** (common surname; the current FY2025 signer is a different person). Flagging for confirmation, not asserting a conflict.

## 10. Bull vs. Bear

**Bull case**
- Real, fast growth (sales +122.5% in 2024, +18.1% in 2025); ~Rp81 bn SOPHI backlog gives near-term visibility.
- Genuine sector tailwind (health budget, JKN, TKDN, ~8.7% market CAGR); credible global principals (Medtronic et al.).
- **Better underwriting (BRI Danareksa)** than the Sucor small-caps; long 30-month ESA lock aligns staff; deep government/regulatory know-how on the board.

**Bear case**
- **~96% government concentration**, **tender-lumpy** revenue, **no recurring contracts**; **heavy leverage** (interest 46% of operating profit); **chronic negative working capital**.
- Margin-dilutive growth (gross 51%→36%); **book value/leverage flattered by a pre-IPO land revaluation**; **no hard founder lock-up**; **demanding ~23–26× P/E**.
- Governance optics (ex-regulator as "independent" commissioner); FY2024/2023 restated.

1. **The SOPHI/IsDB tender economics** — exact remaining backlog, recognition schedule, margins, and the *pipeline* of new tenders (the entire growth case rests here).

3. **Restatement reason** for FY2024/2023; confirm auditor "Juninho Widjaja" is unrelated to the owners.

6. **The JV** — counterparty, nature, and sustainability of the Rp4.10 bn equity income.
7. **Live comparables** (IRRA, OMED) multiples at publication date; dividend policy/history.

forensicMd:
## Thesis

PT Prodia Diagnostic Line Tbk (PRDL, brand Proline) is a small, founder-controlled in-vitro diagnostics (IVD) manufacturer inside the Prodia group, raising roughly Rp62 bn at the cap by issuing 522,900,000 new primary shares (30.00% of post-IPO capital) at Rp100–Rp120 per share. The defining feature is *what the money is for*: the IPO is primarily a debt-repair exercise, with a fixed Rp35,666,666,667 — the majority of net proceeds — earmarked to repay bank debt taken on for a 2024 capacity build that collided with a revenue collapse. This is effectively a second IDX listing of the Prodia group, alongside the already-listed PT Prodia Widyahusada Tbk (PRDA). The analytical read is that PRDL is cheap in absolute terms (~10–12× trailing P/E, ~1.4× P/BV) but that discount is *earned*: extreme earnings volatility, severe channel concentration, a pre-IPO restatement and auditor change, and a thin, illiquid float.

## How the business works

PRDL manufactures IVD reagents and diagnostic instruments (clinical chemistry, hematology, immunology, biomolecular) under the Proline and DiaSys brands, classified under KBLI 21015. It is a standalone single entity with no subsidiaries, so net profit equals owner profit with no minority interest. Crucially, it is not a captive intra-group sales channel: it sells overwhelmingly through third-party distributors and OEM partners rather than to the Prodia lab network, which is structurally cleaner on the revenue side but concentrates risk in that external distributor channel. Distribution reaches roughly 370 kabupaten/kota and 7,600+ end-users (hospitals, puskesmas, public and private labs), with a small workforce of 81 (2025). The revenue mix is shown in a separate card.

## The offering & ownership

This is a 100% primary offering — all 522,900,000 shares are newly issued, with no selling shareholders and no disclosed greenshoe. Because the issuance-cost line is blank ("•%") in the preliminary prospectus, net proceeds are *back-solved* from the prospectus's own allocation arithmetic — the fixed Rp35.667 bn debt item equals 62.57% of net, implying net proceeds of roughly Rp57.0 bn at the cap and a biaya emisi near 9.2% of gross. Price sensitivity is severe and asymmetric: since debt repayment is a fixed Rupiah amount, a weak book at the floor would push it to ~76.6% of net, squeezing growth (capex plus working capital) to roughly Rp11 bn. Ownership stays firmly with the founder group — Prodia Utama and PRDA together hold ~63% post-IPO, with Diasys at 7%; the cap table is a separate card.

## Governance & related parties

Control rests collectively with six Prodia founders (each 16.67% of Prodia Utama), the same group that controls listed PRDA — so this is a group monetisation/restructuring, not an independent company; cross-reading PRDA's disclosures is warranted, as is verifying the named Independent Commissioner's genuine independence given the tight control. The pivotal relationship is the *triple dependency on Diasys Diagnostic Systems GmbH (Germany)*: simultaneously a 10%-pre / 7%-post shareholder, the technology licensor, and a reagent/raw-material supplier. The licence (since April 2011, recently extended 13 April 2026 to 17 April 2033) lets PRDL manufacture reagents under Diasys supervision for licence fees; the Euro-denominated payable (Rp628 m at YE2025) confirms ongoing purchases. The extension de-risks the near term, but core reagent technology resting on one foreign related party is a structural risk, and working-capital proceeds may partly flow back to Diasys. Other related-party links are mostly cost-side (notably renting its building from Grhanis); related-party *revenue* is immaterial.

## Reading the financials

The headline 2025 result — revenue Rp74.37 bn (+26.8%) and net profit Rp16.99 bn (+69.9%) — must not be read off a cherry-picked base. 2023 is an anomalous peak (revenue +301.9%, profit +1,809%) that was ~77% driven by a single BUMN distributor, PT Rajawali Nusindo (Rp86.07 bn), almost certainly a government health-program order that did not repeat; when it collapsed, 2024 revenue halved (−47.5%) and profit fell −72.1%. Even the 2025 recovery leaves the business ~33% below its 2023 revenue peak, and Rajawali Nusindo remains ~33% of revenue (top-3 channels ~54%). This is the company's own stated #1 risk — dependence on lumpy government health spending — and it makes earnings inherently low-visibility. Meanwhile non-current assets roughly doubled to Rp148.9 bn on debt funding for the Cikarang build, cash collapsed 63% in 2025 to Rp10.8 bn, and DSCR fell to 1.95 in 2024 against a ≥1 covenant — the deleveraging rationale for the IPO. The structural quality item is the audit cluster: FY2025 carries an unmodified EY (KAP Purwanto Susanti dan Surja) opinion with revenue recognition as the Key Audit Matter, but FY2024/2023 were audited by a *different* firm (KAP HHES) and the FY2024 statements were *restated/reissued* (Emphasis of Matter). An auditor change (HHES→EY) stacked with a restatement of the very revenue line that is the KAM is the top diligence item; the restatement reason should be established before relying on a clean "audited by EY" line.

## Valuation

On an absolute basis PRDL is cheap — roughly 10–12× trailing earnings and ~1.4× book — the opposite of a premium deal. The analytical caution is that the discount is appropriate rather than an opportunity: the multiple is only as reliable as the assumed normal earnings power, and 2025's Rp16.99 bn, the 3-year average (~Rp20.9 bn), and the unrepeatable 2023 peak imply very different P/E lenses. Post-money ROE also compresses toward ~12% as equity roughly doubles. The natural live comparable is PRDA, its own listed affiliate, plus other IDX healthcare/med-device names — those current multiples should be pulled at publication before any relative cheap/expensive call. The full multiples table is a separate card.

## Bottom line

PRDL offers real positives that the structural story partly offsets: healthy gross margins (61–68%), the Prodia brand and listed-affiliate parentage, a Big-4 EY audit, the Diasys licence secured to 2033, a genuine government-health-spending tailwind (APBN 2026 health budget Rp244 tn; the CKG screening program), and — notably — a *materially stronger* lock-up than peers, with a hard 8-month POJK 25/2017 undertaking on all three pre-IPO holders (70% of post-IPO capital) plus a 12-month control commitment from the six UBOs. The ESA is generous and aligning but a real cash cost: up to 36,603,000 shares (7% of offered) given free to permanent staff, paid from internal cash, with a 12-month lock-up. Against that sits a debt-repair use of proceeds that leaves ~Rp58 bn of bank debt outstanding, volatile and government-dependent earnings off an unrepeatable peak, an unresolved 2024 restatement, capacity built ahead of demand, thin cash, and a tiny illiquid near-par float. The verdict is consistent throughout: cheap, but cheap for documented reasons.
